observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to coordinate with the Market Supervisor and obtain the complete subsidiary schedules to support the recorded balance of Account Receivable account amounting to P480,837.18.
observations.managementAction: Not Data gathering efforts of the Accounting Office were still ongoing to ascertain the details of the beginning balance of Accounts Receivable.
observations.reason: Data gathering efforts of the Accounting Office were still ongoing to ascertain the details of the beginning balance of Accounts Receivable.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Officer-in-Charge (OIC) – MWS to: a. Provide all the MWS customers in Barangay Pacheco, Magallanes, with the detailed breakdown of their monthly water bills for the months of August 2023 to December 2023 and henceforth, to see to it that all the MWS customers in the Municipality shall receive a copy of their monthly billing statements to ensure transparency as to their monthly water consumption and monthly water bill; b. Strictly enforce the policy on imposition of surcharge to MWS customers who were not able to settle their monthly bills on due date or within the first 15 days of the following month, as prescribed in Sections 5F.01 and Section 5F.02 of the 2018 Revenue Code of the Municipality of Magallanes; and c. Strictly enforce the policy on disconnection of water service to MWS customers who were not able to settle their monthly bills within 30 days after the due date, as prescribed in Section 5F.04 of the same Revenue Code, as a measure to compel the MWS customers to pay their obligations to MWS on time and minimize the accumulation of delinquent accounts.
observations.managementAction: Management commented that the OIC- MWS Supervisor will issue Demand Letters and will exert their best effort to collect the identified amounts. Provision of allowance for impairment has been coordinated with the Municipal Accountant if their efforts to collect and settle the closed and temporary-closed account were not met.
observations.reason:
Detailed breakdown of the subject billing could not be provided as the existing system could only produce the lumpsum amount of the subject billings.
The foregoing could not be addressed as the system developer had passed away.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor designate a focal person/committee who will assist the Municipal Agriculturist in the completion of accreditation of the Municipality’s slaughterhouse with the NMIS and in ensuring the availability of licensed and trained meat inspector and other necessary personnel, in order and that the slaughterhouse facility can be operated as soon as possible so that additional income under Section 5B.01 of the 2018 Revenue Code of the Municipality could be generated and to guarantee the safety of all the meat and meat products to be distributed and sold within the Municipality for human consumption.
observations.managementAction: The concerned Municipal officials had already obtained an approved plan from MERALCO. The plan is yet to be obtained.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to
validate the noted discrepancy and immediately prepare the necessary adjusting
journal entries to properly record the receipt of loan proceeds from the loan granted by
the LBP with gross amount of P28,907,200.00. Thereafter, submit the duly signed JEV
to the Audit Team for review and validation.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
1. Municipal Treasurer, as General Services Officer, to:
a. Submit the completed IIRUP and all supporting documents, including the
required Certification from the Appraisal and Disposal Committee, to the
Municipal Accountant pertaining to the disposal of four unserviceable
properties which are necessary for the derecognition thereof from the books
of accounts;
b. Coordinate with the Appraisal and Disposal Committee regarding the disposal
of the six unserviceable properties valued at P703,213.20;
2. Appraisal and Disposal Committee to
a. Explain to the Audit Team the non-adherence to the provisions of the Revised
Manual on Disposal of Government Properties on the disposal of four
unserviceable properties with total carrying value of P1,727,729.25;
b. Strictly comply with the procedures and requirements set forth in COA and DBM
JMC No. 2024-1, dated January 30, 2024, regarding the disposal of properties;
and
3. Municipal Accountant, upon the receipt of accomplished IIRUP and all the
documentary requirements pertaining to the subject properties that were already
disposed, evaluate the completeness and validity thereof and immediately record
the necessary entries to derecognize the same from the books. Thereafter, submit
a duly signed copy of Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to the Audit Team, along with
the copy of accomplished IIRUP and other supporting documents, for review and
validation.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
a. Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Office (LDRRMO) to investigate on
the existence of excess welfare goods for distribution amounting to P15,933.50 and
submit the correct RPCI and to the Municipal Accountant for proper recording of
necessary adjustment in the books of accounts, if warranted;
b. Municipal Accountant to coordinate with the LDRRMO pertaining to the afore-
mentioned discrepancy and submission of the required documents. Upon receipt,
review and validate the same and record the necessary adjustment in the books, if
warranted, and furnish the Audit Team with the duly signed copy of JEV and
justification/explanation letters for review and validation; and
c. Municipal Accountant to properly prepare SLCs and provide the Inventory
Custodians with the weighted average unit cost of inventory items under their
custody at year-end and ensure that all recorded inventories are with corresponding
RPCI.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
a. Officer In Charge (OIC) of the MWS to implement additional measures to ensure the
collection of outstanding amounts from delinquent MWS customers; and
b. Municipal Accountant to coordinate with the OIC of the MWS for the assessment of
probability of collections to determine whether an allowance for impairment should
be provided, if warranted.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
1. Municipal Treasurer, as General Services Officer, to:
a. Renew the registration of the 22 Municipality-owned vehicles with the LTO by
obtaining the required documentation for registration and fixing any


discrepancies in the information, including but not limited to the wrong vehicle
body type and a different owner's name in the Official Receipt and Certificate of
Registration (OR/CR);
b. Ensure that all motor vehicles are covered by the General Insurance Fund (GIF)
of the Government Service Insurance System (GSIS) by coordinating with
concerned departments to collect all necessary documents for the insurance of
the Municipality's vehicles;
c. Secure Deeds of Donation, MOA or any related documents granting the
Municipality the ownership or right to use the vehicles which are received from
PGC and facilitate the transfer of registration thereof under the name of the
Municipality;
d. Coordinate with the LTO for the issuance of the plate numbers for the eight
vehicles and, if feasible, assign a specific individual to promptly handle the
process.
2. Municipal Accountant to:
a. Locate the copy of the Deed of Donation or coordinate with the PGC for the copy
thereof pertaining to one unit of Van/Ambulance which cost and corresponding
depreciation expenses was recorded in the books since receipt thereof on
CY 2002. Otherwise, make the necessary adjusting entry to dropped from the
books the related cost and accumulated depreciation and submit the duly signed
Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to the Audit Team for review and validation; and
b. Upon receipt of the Deeds of Donation, coordinate with the Appraisal Committee
for the Fair Market Value (FMV) at the time of donation of the motor vehicle
received from PGC and immediately record the same in the books. Thereafter,
furnish the Audit Team with a copy of the duly signed JEV and copy of the Deeds
of Donation for review and validation.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Administrator, HRMO
and other responsible Officials to:
a. Explain why the digitalization project remained idle and not operational, and the
specific actions to be taken by the Municipality to ensure that the amount of
P898,000.00 is not wasted, otherwise, a Notice of Disallowance (ND) will be issued
accordingly;
b. Explore and implement alternative measures to promptly hire competent IT
personnel, or designate existing municipal employees, if available, who possess the
necessary skills to operate and manage the software in order to achieve the project’s
intended purposes.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
1. Market Supervisor to execute an amended contract with the stallholders in the eatery
section who personally funded the construction of the building and were granted a
five-year waiver on rental fees. The revised contract should explicitly outline the
terms of the agreement, including, but not limited to, the ownership of the
constructed building and any other relevant provisions; and
2. Municipal Treasurer to instruct the designated Market Collector to ensure the
collection of the corresponding penalties from defaulting vendors, as specified in
the order of payment prepared by the Market Supervisor.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer to:
a. Deposit all collections intact and daily or on the next banking day with the
Municipality’s depository bank to ensure that the collections are properly
safeguarded against the risk of possible loss or unauthorized use, pursuant to
Section 32 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I; and
b. Assign an official vehicle and guarantee its accessibility to enable the Municipal
Treasurer’s Office to deposit the daily collections at the depository bank on a timely
basis.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendations and Management concurred to:
Require the Inventory, Appraisal and Disposal Committee to perform its duties and functions, specifically on the annual conduct of the actual physical count of all PPE to come up with accurate and reliable RPCPPE and relieve the OMA in the preparation of the said report to facilitate proper reconciliation of PPE records; and
Avail the one-time cleansing of PPE account balances prescribed by COA Circular No. 2020-006 to establish PPE balances that are verifiable as to existence, condition and accountability.
We likewise recommended and the OMA agreed to:
Prepare and maintain complete and updated PPELCs, RPLCs and Local Road Network Ledger Card for all PPE accounts based on the format prescribed by the MNGAS for LGUs and Annex D of COA Circular No. 2015-008, respectively; and
Immediately prepare the necessary adjusting entries for PPE to correct the recognition of semi-expendable properties and henceforth, consider appropriate account titles as prescribed by COA Circular No. 2024-006 dated March 14, 2024.
We further recommended and the MGSO likewise agreed to prepare and maintain complete and updated PCs and Local Road Network Property Card based on the format prescribed by the MNGAS for LGUs and Annex E of COA Circular No. 2015-008, respectively.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Require the MGSO, in coordination with the concerned offices, to conduct complete physical inventory count of all inventories and prepare the RPCI semestrally based on the results of the actual count with complete details and description of the inventoried items;
Instruct the OMA and MGSO to: (i) analyze the discrepancies noted and reconcile their records; (ii) effect the necessary adjustments in the books of accounts, if warranted; and (iii) maintain complete and updated SCs and SLCs for each inventory item; and
Remind the concerned offices to submit the pertinent documents to OMA and MGSO, immediately after the distribution of the inventory items, to facilitate timely preparation of SSMIs as a basis for recording corresponding expense accounts, and ensure to retain copies said documents for monitoring and recording purposes.

We reiterated our recommendations and Management agreed to compel the OMA and the MTO to conduct a comprehensive and regular reconciliation of the MGM’s cash accounts and effect the necessary adjustments to come up with the correct balances on both records.
We likewise recommended and the MT also agreed to ensure the completeness and correctness of the entries recorded in the cashbooks and practice daily recording, updating and balancing of collections and deposits in the cashbooks.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation and Management agreed to:
Constantly monitor and control its disbursements from TFs to ensure that monies held in trust are used solely for their intended purpose; and
Require the immediate settlement of long outstanding receivables to augment the cash deficiency under the TF.
We recommended and the MGSO agreed to maintain the SPC and the RegSPI while the OMA prepare and maintain the SPLC in accordance with the provisions of COA Circulars Nos. 2022-004 and 2024-006.

We recommended and Management agreed to remind the OMA to ensure that only unexpended balances from the QRF and LDRRMF-MOOE are transferred to the STF and recognized in the TF books as Trust Liability-DRRMF at year-end, in compliance with COA Circular No. 2012-002.

We recommended and Management agreed to direct the MDRRMO to submit a comprehensive monthly Report on Utilization of DRRMF duly certified by the Municipal Accountant, on or before the 15th day of the succeeding month to the AT as prescribed in COA Circular No. 2012-002.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Fast-track the completion of the ongoing and unimplemented PPAs and ensure that the subsequent projects to be included in the AIP are implemented as scheduled; and
Meticulously plan the implementation phase of the PPAs under the 20% DF to optimally utilize the fund and attain the desired benefits and services to its constituents without unnecessary delay.
We recommended and Management agreed to require the LSB to review and approve the SEF Budget for the ensuing year not later than October 31 of the current year.

We recommended and Management committed to pursue follow-up on previous inquiry and initiate necessary actions to validate the long-standing negative balance of the Due to GSIS account which may include coordination with the GSIS for account reconciliation and further investigation of prior year balances.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Ensure prompt and full remittance of all mandatory contributions and loan amortizations withheld from employees to the HDMF within the prescribed period, in compliance with existing laws, rules and regulations; and
Investigate and reconcile the reported under-remittance for CY 2024 amounting to ₱44,700.02, and immediately take the necessary action to settle any deficiencies.

We recommended and Management agreed to ensure prompt and full remittance of all mandatory contributions withheld from employees to the PhilHealth within the prescribed period, in compliance with existing laws, rules and regulations.

We reiterated our prior year’s audit recommendation with modifications and Management agreed to:
Require the GFPS to fully comply with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2021-008 by using the created and assigned responsibility center codes as additional reference in its GPB and GAD AR;
Remind the OMA to ensure correct and reliable recording in the SLs for the GFPS and other GAD-related financial reports; and
Instruct the MBO to indicate the RCs in the GAD Monitoring and Utilization Report to facilitate ease of reconciliation and ensure a more integrated and comprehensive accounting, monitoring, and reporting of the GAD programs and funds.

1.10 We recommended and the Management agreed that the Municipal
Accountant prepare an adjusting journal entry for the
unreleased/unclaimed checks issued restoring the Cash in Bank–
Local Currency, Current Account and the recognition/restoration of
liabilities account to faithfully present the financial position of the
Municipality as year-end. Moving forward, require the Municipal
Treasurer to submit to the Municipal Accounting Office at each
reporting period a report on the unreleased/unclaimed checks for the
proper recording by the latter of the said transactions in the books
of accounts.

2.8 We recommended and the Management agreed that:
2.8.1 The Municipal Accountant ensure that the basis of the
recognition of the Real Property Tax (RPT) and the Special
Education Tax (SET) Receivables accounts at the beginning
of the year should be the Certified List of Tax Payers and
Taxes Due as prepared by the Municipal Treasury Office
(MTO), in compliance with the provisions of Section 20,
Chapter 3 of the New Government Accounting System (NGAS)
Manual for Local Government Units (LGUs), Volume I.
2.8.2 The Municipal Accountant, in coordination with the MTO,
ensure that discounts on advance and prompt payments of
taxes due will be recognized in accordance with Section 22,
Chapter 3 of the NGAS Manual for LGUs, Volume I and COA
Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015.
2.8.3 The Municipal Accountant, with the assistance of the MTO
and the Municipal Assessor Office, make the necessary
adjustments in the books of accounts to properly record the
balances of the RPT and the SET Receivables accounts and
the corresponding deferred and discount on advance
payments accounts in accordance with Section 22, Chapter 3
of the NGAS Manual for LGUs, Volume I and COA Circular
No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015.

3.22 We recommended and the Management agreed that:
3.22.1 The Municipal Accountant continually issue written
notices/demand letters to the concerned accountable officer,
both organic and non-organic personnel of the Municipal to
require the immediate settlement thereof pursuant to COA
Circular No. 2012-004 dated November 28, 2012 and/or impose
the withholding of the salaries of those presently employed
personnel and elected officials as provided for under
paragraphs 5.1.3 of COA Circular No. 97-002 dated
February 10, 1997.
3.22.2 The Municipal Accountant make representations with COA
Central Office to follow-up the status of Liquidation Reports
for the cash advances pertaining to Confidential Fund in
calendar years 2008 to 2014.
3.22.3 The Municipal Accountant continually act on the request for
authority to write off unliquidated cash advances and other
receivable accounts for deceased accountable officers and
the application of the proper disposition and derecognition of
dormant accounts, particularly those accounts which are no
longer traceable, in accordance with COA Circular
No. 2023-008 dated August 17, 2023.

4.15 We recommended and the Management agreed that:
4.15.1 The Inspectorate Committee conduct the required annual
physical count of PPE in compliance with COA Circular
No. 2002-003 dated June 20, 2002.
4.15.2 The MGS Officer coordinate with the Accounting Office to
determine the items to be included in the RPCPPE and with
the Inspectorate Committee on the details and other relevant
information to ensure the proper accomplishment of the
RPCPPE based on the guidelines provided under COA
Circular Nos. 2002-003 dated June 20, 2002 and 2020-006
dated January 31, 2020.
4.15.3 The MGS Officer maintain the required Property Cards or its
equivalent to record all acquisitions, depreciations, disposals
and other information about the PPE to facilitate the
reconciliation of any noted discrepancy between the records
of the Accounting and the General Services Offices.
4.15.4 Facilitate the application of a one-time cleansing of PPE
accounts in accordance with COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated
January 31, 2020; and
4.15.5 Consider increasing the manpower complement of the MGSO
or assigning personnel whose task is focused in
accomplishing the required property documents and
facilitating the reconciliation of the records of the Accounting
and General Services Offices, in compliance with COA
Circular 2002-003 dated June 20, 2002.

5.9 We recommended and the Management agreed that:
5.9.1 The Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer
examine each of these funds received and recorded under the
Due to LGU account to determine any unimplemented
programs and forward this information to the concerned
implementing office, otherwise return these
unimplemented/excess funds to the Source Agency as
required in COA Circular No. 94-013 dated December 13, 1994.
5.9.2 The Municipal Accountant/Municipal Treasurer communicate
with LGU Baguio, as the source agency of the funds received
in the amount of ₱100,000.00, for the identification of the
purpose of the funds received, otherwise, return the same to
the source agency.

6.7 We recommended and the Management agreed that the concerned
municipal employees and barangay officials refund the excessive
claims of daily traveling expenses totaling ₱231,770.00, without
prejudice to the issuance of the required notice of disallowance
pursuant to COA Circular No. 2012-003 dated October 29, 2012.

7.9 We recommended and the Management agreed that the assigned
personnel submit the paid disbursement vouchers on the grant of
cash advance for Confidential Fund and their required supporting
documents to the concerned COA Audit Team Leader within seven
(7) days from release of check in compliance with the COA-DBM-
DILG-GCG-DND Joint Circular No. 2015-01 dated January 8, 2015.

8.7 We recommended and the Management agreed that the Local School
Board (LSB) prepare, submit and post the Quarterly and Annual SEF
Utilization reports within the prescribed period to ensure compliance
with Item 6.1 of DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 1, s. 2017 dated
January 19, 2017.

9.13 We recommended and the Management agreed that:
9.13.1 The concerned committee of the Gender and Development
(GAD) Focal Point System establish and maintain an agency-
wide GAD database containing an improved sex-
disaggregated data and gender statistics and utilize these
data in the conduct of gender analysis to ensure the
alignment of the established gender issues and gaps in the
GAD planning, budgeting, programming and reporting, in
compliance with the PCW/NEDA/DBM Joint Circular
No. 2012-01 and Item 5.2 of PCW Memorandum Circular (MC)
No. 2018-04 dated May 4, 2018.
9.13.2 The Municipality set/update Gender and Development
Code/Agenda within the three-year term of the local chief
executive, as prescribed under PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint
Memorandum Circular (JMC) No. 2013-01 to address priority
gender issues that are included in the GPB.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Treasurer/GSO to strictly observe the pertinent provisions of the MNGAS for LGUs, that all future disposals of real property of the Municipality should have an IIRUP and other required supporting documents, and to submit the same to the Municipal Accountant to serve as proper basis for dropping/derecognizing the PPE from the books of accounts.
We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Treasurer/GSO to submit IIRUP and other disposal documents to the Municipal Accountant to serve as proper basis for dropping/derecognizing the PPE from the books of accounts.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Treasurer/GSO already coordinated with the Audit Team regarding the issues on the two demolished Bureau of Internal Revenue (BIR) building. A request letter for authority to derecognize the same from the books of accounts duly supported with IIRUP and pictures of scrap materials, were submitted on March 17, 2025.
observations.reason:
Not The Appraisal and Disposal Committee was not yet aware of the revised procedures disposal.
While no reason was given for the inability of the Municipal Treasurer/GSO to submit IIRUP and other disposal documents to the Municipal Accountant.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant and other concerned officials to properly evaluate the repairs and/or improvements done or to be done to the Municipality’s assets to determine whether it extends the useful life of the original property and add these costs to the carrying value of the PPE, once recognition criteria per IPSAS No. 17 are met, otherwise, record these repairs/improvements in the appropriate Repairs and Maintenance account.
observations.managementAction: Adjusting entry was made as evidenced by JEV No. 100-2024-06-175b dated June 30, 2024.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to validate the submitted monitoring report prepared by the Municipal Social Welfare and Development Office by requiring them to secure the copy of death certificates and statements of account from the funeral service provider and also to make the necessary adjusting entry to properly record the prepayments for the cost of services not yet provided. Thereafter, submit a copy of the JEV duly supported with the afore-mentioned source documents to the Audit Team for review and validation.
observations.managementAction: Adjusting entry was made as evidenced by JEV No. 175c dated June 30, 2023 to record the cost of services not yet provided as of December 31, 2023.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, being the special disbursing officer for CF, ensure that the release of the quarterly allocation for CF are to be made as early as first month of every quarter to enable disbursements of CF on schedule in accordance with the approved Physical and Financial Plan and to facilitate the liquidation of the cash advances for CF within the prescribed period of 30 days after every quarter as provided in Section 6.2.2 of the COA-DBM-DILG-GCG-DND JMC No. 2015-01. We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant or authorized representative to submit the cash advance disbursement voucher and the required supporting documents for CF to the COA ATL within seven days after the release of check to enable the auditor to conduct prompt post-audit of the same and ensure compliance with Section 6.1.8.4 of the aforementioned JMC.
observations.managementAction: The recommendation was promptly implemented, beginning with the Cash Advance for CF for the third quarter of CY 2024.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the: a. Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to pay the amounts due to regular officials and employees and honoraria of Contract of Service personnel thru Automated Payroll System and/or by checks directly to the payees, and to evaluate the designation and bonding of additional disbursing officer/s to reduce the cash advances being handled by the aforementioned disbursing officer;
 b. Municipal Accountant to refrain from granting additional cash advance to the disbursing officer if he/she still has an unliquidated previous cash advance; c. Municipal Accountant to properly orient all the disbursing officers to provide documentary evidence of payment and to report or liquidate their cash advance as soon as its purpose has been served; d. Municipal Accountant to properly orient all the disbursing officers to maintain a cashbook and to update and reconcile the same regularly with accounting records to ensure that all transactions of the disbursing officer are completely and properly recorded in the cashbook. Likewise, require all the disbursing officers to record the liquidation of cash advance in the cashbook on the actual transaction date to ensure correct reporting of unliquidated cash advance balance as of specific date; and e. Municipal Accountant to record the payments for allowances of non-contract of service personnel, cash assistance to private individuals, subsistence allowance, prizes, operating expenses of PNP personnel assigned in the Municipality, and clothing allowance to appropriate accounts prescribed under the Revised Chart of Accounts for LGUs to ensure the fair presentation of the Statement of Financial Performance of the Municipality.
observations.managementAction:
Direct payment to the payees already in place.
The Municipal Accountant is closely coordinating with the Municipal Treasurer to monitor cash advance balances and prevent overlapping advances of regular officials and employees.
The Municipal Accountant is closely coordinating with the Municipal Treasurer on the proper documentation of evidence of payments and the use of Report on Disbursement as soon as the cash advance’s purpose is served.
The Accountant reminded and oriented the concerned Municipal Treasurer staff on the proper maintenance of cashbook for cash advances.
The Municipal Accountant is closely coordinating with the Municipal Treasurer to monitor cash advance balances and prevent overlapping advances of disbursing officers.
The Municipal Accountant is closely coordinating with the Municipal Treasurer on the proper documentation of evidence of payments and the use of Report on Disbursement as soon as the cash advance’s purpose is served.
The liquidation of cash advances on CY 2024 were recorded under the appropriate accounts.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to maintain the subsidiary ledgers of the unexpended balances of LDRRMF by year of transfer to facilitate the monitoring of the five-year period in which the funds shall remain in the Special Trust Fund and the reversion of any remaining balance to the General Fund after said period.
observations.managementAction: A journal entry was made as evidenced by JEV No. 42a dated June 30, 2023.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor issue an appropriate executive order for the re-organization of the LSB of Magallanes which shall be expanded membership composed of the required members only as provided in Section 98(b)(3) of the Local Government Code. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the concerned LSB and Municipal Budget Officer to submit a written justification why the disbursements totaling P3,426,558.73 charged to SEF for CY 2023 should not be disallowed despite the absence of the duly approved SEF Budget for the year. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the LSB of Magallanes to strictly observe the prescribed timeline for the review and approval of the SEF Budget of the Municipality as provided in Section 5.5 of the DepEd-DBM-DILG JC No. 1 to enable the timely submission to concerned Provincial LSB of proposals not funded in the approved SEF Budget of the Municipality for possible augmentation and additional funding support from the former. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the LSB of Magallanes to include ECCD Programs in the priority programs and projects to be funded under the annual SEF Budget of the Municipality in the succeeding years to ensure compliance with Section 3.4 of the DepEd-DBM-DILG JC No. 1 and Section 7 (b) of RA No. 10410. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the LSB of Magallanes to refrain from utilizing the SEF Budget for expenditures which do not relate with the priority programs and projects that are allowed to be charged to SEF.
observations.managementAction:
The Local School Board (LSB) of Magallanes which shall be expanded membership of the LSB was issued on December 23, 2024 (Executive Order No. 47, Series of 2024).
The CY 2024 budget for SEF was approved by LSB on October 03, 2024.
The CY 2024 SEF budget included ECCD allocation for supplies and materials for ECCD amounting to P100,000.00.
On CY 2024, all disbursements charged to the SEF were in accordance with the prescribed guidelines.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the: a. Municipal Accountant to ensure that all progress and final payments to suppliers and contractors are supported with complete documentation, to avoid suspension in audit; b. Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to ensure that the bidding documents contain Instruction to Bidders, Bid Data Sheet, General Conditions of the Contract (GCC) and the Special Conditions of the Contract (SCC) to clearly and adequately define the obligations, duties and/or functions of the winning Bidder, in accordance with the Philippine Bidding Documents issued by the GPPB; c. BAC to ensure that the submitted Required Forms of the contractor contain the mandatory provisions stated in Item 6.0 of GPPB Circular No. 04-2020 and disqualify non-complying bidders; d. Municipal Engineer to require the contractors to prepare and submit the As-Built plans for all infrastructure projects awarded to them upon reaching 95 percent completion for their review, and to not issue a Certificate of Completion unless the As-Built plan has been approved, to avoid suspension in audit; and e. BAC and the Technical Working Group (TWG) to conduct bid evaluation and post-qualification and prepare bid evaluation and post-qualification reports and notice of post-qualification in accordance with the RIRR of RA 9184, to be attached to the disbursement vouchers submitted to COA for audit.
observations.managementAction: Documentary requirements for the procurement of infrastructure projects for the year were not fully complied. JEV No. 01 (Operation of Market) was made on June 01, 2023 to recognize Trust Liability for refundable Market Award Fee as of December 31, 2022. (Note: Various follow-up and remedial actions were undertaken including issuance of checklist and requests to BAC/engineer to comply.)
observations.reason: Documentary requirements prescribed in COA Circular No. 2012-001 were not fully complied by the Municipality.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer, as the Property Officer, to renew the registration with the LTO of the 15 vehicles owned by the Municipality in compliance with Section 5 of Batas Pambansa Bilang 74 and follow-up the release of the number plates of the ten vehicles. Also, to insure all the Motor Vehicles under the GIF of the GSIS and to investigate why the Official Receipt/Certificate of Registration of four motorcycles were not registered in the name of the Municipality of Magallanes.
observations.managementAction: We will continue to gather all the required documents to proceed with the process of LTO registration for all the vehicles. According to the supplier, documents are incomplete due to unclear copy of application of insurance and LTO registration. Prepared Deed of Sale for a couple of vehicles listed but documentary evidence on the results of the investigation was not submitted to the Audit Team. The General Services Office is following up with the LTO and suppliers.
observations.reason: The information required in the submitted reports was incomplete due to lack of supporting documents; processing of renewal and securing of plate numbers and insurance remains pending.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant and
Municipal Treasurer/GSO to refer to the COA the issues on the two demolished
buildings with total cost of P825,711.12 which did not have the required IIRUP.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Treasurer/GSO
to strictly observe the pertinent provisions of the MNGAS for LGUs, that all future
disposals of real property of the Municipality should have an IIRUP and other required
supporting documents, and to submit the same to the Municipal Accountant to serve as
proper basis for dropping/derecognizing the PPE from the books of accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to record
the appropriate adjusting entry in the books of accounts to correct the misstatements in
the PPE and Government Equity balances due to recording of the payment for the cost
of rehabilitation of water pipeline distribution system and water connection amounting to
P2,351,005.62 in CY 2023 to expense instead of PPE.

We reiterated our prior year’s audit recommendation that the Municipal Mayor
instruct the Municipal Accountant and other concerned officials to properly evaluate
the repairs and/or improvements done or to be done to the Municipality’s assets to
determine whether it extends the useful life of the original property and add these
costs to the carrying value of the PPE, once recognition criteria per IPSAS 17 are met,
otherwise, record these repairs/improvements in the appropriate Repairs and
Maintenance account.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct Municipal Accountant to
coordinate with the Market Supervisor and obtain the complete subsidiary schedules
to support the recorded balance of Account Receivable account amounting to
P480,837.18.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to
validate the submitted monitoring report prepared by the Municipal Social Welfare and
Development Office by requiring them to secure the copy of death certificates and
statements of account from the funeral service provider and also to make the necessary
adjusting entry to properly record the prepayments for the cost of services not yet
provided. Thereafter, submit a copy of the Journal Entry Voucher duly supported with
the afore-mentioned source documents to the Audit Team for review and validation.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, being the special disbursing officer for CF,
and the Municipal Accountant to ensure that the release of the quarterly allocation for
CF are to be made as early as first month of every quarter to enable disbursements of
CF on schedule in accordance with the approved Physical and Financial Plan and to
facilitate the liquidation of the cash advances for CF within the prescribed period of 30
days after every quarter as provided in Section 6.2.2 of the COA-DBM-DILG-GCG-DND
JMC No. 2015-01.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant or
authorized representative to submit the cash advance disbursement voucher and the
required supporting documents for CF to the COA ATL within seven days after the
release of check to enable the auditor to conduct prompt post-audit of the same and
ensure compliance with Section 6.1.8.4 of the abovementioned JMC.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
a) Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to evaluate the feasibility of
payment of all the amounts due to regular officials and employees and honoraria
of Contract of Service personnel thru Automated Payroll System and/or by
checks directly to the payees, and to evaluate and submit recommendations to
the Municipal Mayor on the designation and bonding of additional disbursing
officer/s to reduce the cash advances being handled by the aforementioned
disbursing officer;
b) Municipal Accountant to refrain from granting additional cash advance to the
disbursing officer if he/she still has an unliquidated previous cash advance;
c) Municipal Accountant to properly orient all the disbursing officers to provide the
date of payment in all the official receipts, sales invoices, payrolls, and other
documentary evidence of payment and to report or liquidate their cash advance
as soon as its purpose has been served to facilitate the timely verification of the
validity of the Report of Disbursement and its supporting documents and the
timely recording of the liquidation of cash advance in the books of accounts;
d) Municipal Accountant to properly orient all the disbursing officers to maintain a
cashbook and to update and reconcile the same regularly with accounting
records to ensure that all transactions of the disbursing officer are completely
and properly recorded in the cashbook. Likewise, require all the disbursing
officers to record the liquidation of cash advance in the cashbook on the actual
transaction date to ensure the correct reporting of the unliquidated cash advance
balance as of specific date; and
e) Municipal Accountant to record the payments for allowances of non-contract of
service personnel, cash assistance to private individuals, subsistence allowance,
prizes, operating expenses of Philippine National Police personnel assigned in
the Municipality, and clothing allowance to appropriate accounts prescribed
under the Revised Chart of Accounts for LGUs to ensure the fair presentation of
the Statement of Financial Performance of the Municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the OIC – MWS Supervisor to:
a) provide all the MWS customers in Barangay Pacheco, Magallanes, with the
detailed breakdown of their monthly water bills for the months of August 2023 to
December 2023 and henceforth, to see to it that all the MWS customers in the
Municipality shall receive a copy of their monthly billing statements to ensure
transparency as to their monthly water consumption and monthly water bill;
b) strictly enforce the policy on imposition of surcharge to MWS customers who were
not able to settle their monthly bills on due date or within the first 15 days of the
following month, as prescribed in Sections 5F.01 and Section 5F.02 of the 2018
Revenue Code of the Municipality of Magallanes; and
c) strictly enforce the policy on disconnection of water service to MWS customers
who were not able to settle their monthly bills within 30 days after the due date, as
prescribed in Section 5F.04 of the same Revenue Code, as a measure to compel
the MWS customers to pay their obligations to MWS on time and minimize the
accumulation of delinquent accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to
maintain the subsidiary ledgers of the unexpended balances of LDDRMF by year of
transfer to facilitate the monitoring of the five-year period in which the funds shall
remain in the Special Trust Fund and the reversion of any remaining balance to the
General Fund after said period.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor issue an appropriate executive order
for the re-organization of the LSB of Magallanes which shall be composed of the
required members only as provided in Section 98(b)(3) of the Local Government
Code.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the concerned LSB and Municipal
Budget Officer to submit a written justification why the disbursements totaling
P3,426,558.73 charged to SEF for CY 2023 should not be disallowed despite the
absence of the duly approved SEF Budget for the year.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Budget Officer
to refrain from incurring additional obligations for SEF until a duly approved SEF
Budget for CY 2024 is obtained to avoid disallowance of transactions in audit.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the LSB of Magallanes to strictly
observe the prescribed timeline for the review and approval of the SEF Budget of
the Municipality as provided in Section 5.5 of the DepEd-DBM-DILG JC No. 1 to
enable the timely submission to concerned Provincial LSB of proposals not
funded in the approved SEF Budget of the Municipality for possible augmentation
and additional funding support.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the LSB of Magallanes to include
ECCD Programs in the priority programs and projects to be funded under the
annual SEF Budget of the Municipality in the succeeding years to ensure
compliance with Section 3.4 of the DepEd-DBM-DILG JC No. 1 and Section 7 (b) of
RA No. 10410.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the LSB of Magallanes to refrain
from utilizing the SEF Budget for expenditures which do not relate with the priority
programs and projects that are allowed to be charged to SEF as provided in
Section 272 of RA No. 7160 and Section 4 of the DepEd-DBM-DILG JC No. 1 to
ensure that the SEF Budget is utilized solely for its intended purpose.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor designate a focal person/committee who
will assist the Municipal Agriculturist in the completion of accreditation of the
Municipality’s slaughterhouse facility with the NMIS and in ensuring the availability
of licensed and trained meat inspector and other necessary personnel, in order that
the slaughterhouse facility can be operated as soon as possible so that additional
income under Section 5B.01 of the 2018 Revenue Code of the Municipality could be
generated and to guarantee the safety of all the meat and meat products to be
distributed and sold within the Municipality for human consumption.

1.10 	We recommended and the Management agreed that the Municipal Accountant, together with the Municipal Assessor, reconcile both their records to make the necessary adjusting entries to record all land owned by the Municipality, in consonance with Section 111 of Presidential Decree 1445 and International Public Sector Accounting Standards (IPSAS) No. 1 and 17.

2.11 	We recommended and the Management agreed that:
2.11.1 The Municipal Accountant prepare the necessary adjusting entries to reclassify the completed projects to its appropriate Property, Plant and Equipment (PPE) accounts in the amount of ₱8,745,422.65 and to recognize the related depreciations. Likewise, encourage the Municipal Accountant to review all ensuing transactions and ensure timely and proper recording of the completed infrastructure projects to its appropriate PPE accounts, in accordance with International Public Sector Accounting Standards (IPSAS) No. 17 and COA Circular            No. 2002-003 dated June 20, 2002.
2.11.2	The Municipal Engineer fast-track the preparation of the procurement and other pertinent documents relating to the projects and submit the same to the Municipal Accounting Office to facilitate recording of said completed projects.

3.9 	We recommended and the Management agreed that the Municipal Accountant prepare the necessary adjusting entries to record the transfer of the unexpended/unobligated balance of the Local Disaster Risk Reduction Management Fund (LDRRMF) for calendar years 2022 and 2023 in the amount of ₱6,157,454.50 and ₱5,214,114.50, respectively, in compliance with Republic Act No. 10121 and COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012, and ensure that future recognition of the unexpended balance of the LDRRMF be recorded in its pertinent calendar year of incurrence.

4.11 	We recommended and the management agreed that the Municipal Accountant prepare the necessary adjusting entries to derecognize the issued Inventory accounts in the amount of ₱5,842,231.30 and to recognize the pertinent expense or government equity accounts, in accordance with COA Circular No. 2022-004 dated May 31, 2022.

We recommended and the Management agreed that the Municipal Accountant:

Prepare the necessary journal entries to adjust the erroneous recording of settlements of prior years’ Accounts Payable amounting to ₱319,942.03 by debiting the Accounts Payable and crediting the Prior Years’ Adjustments/ Government Equity accounts, in accordance with Section 111 (2) of Presidential Decree (PD) No. 1445 and Internal Public Sector Accounting Standards (IPSAS) No. 1.
Facilitate the reversion to the unappropriated surplus of the General Fund, the accounts payable in the amount of ₱234,411.90, which has been outstanding for two years or more and against which no claim, administrative or judicial, has been filed or which is not covered by perfected contracts on record in accordance with Section 98 of Presidential Decree 1445.

6.10 	We recommended and the Management agreed that the Municipal Treasurer coordinate with the Municipal Accountant on the cashbook reconciling items in the amount of ₱645,583.98 to establish the correct balances of the cash in bank account with the records of the Accounting and the Treasury Office. Likewise, require both Offices to conduct regular reconciliation of their records to ensure reconciled balances at report date.

7.10 	We recommended and the Management agreed that the assigned personnel submit the paid disbursement vouchers on the grant of cash advance for Confidential Fund and its required supporting documents to the concerned COA Audit Team Leader within seven (7) working days from the release of check, in compliance with the COA-DBM-DILG-GCG-DND Joint Circular No. 2015-01 dated January 8, 2015.

8.23 	We recommended and the Management agreed that:
8.23.1 The Municipal Accountant issue written notices/demand letters to the concerned accountable officer both organic or non-organic personnel of the Municipal to require the immediate settlement thereof pursuant to COA Circular No. 2012-004 dated       November 28, 2012 and/or impose the withholding of the salaries of those presently employed personnel and elected officials as provided for under paragraphs 5.1.3 of COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997.

observations.recommendation:
1.14 	We recommended and Management agreed to:
Direct the Municipal Accountant to issue written notices/demand letters to the concerned accountable officer to require the immediate settlement thereof pursuant to COA Circular              No. 2012-004 dated November 28, 2012 and/or impose the withholding of the salaries of those presently employed personnel and elected officials as provided for under paragraphs 5.1.3 of COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997.
Require the Municipal Accountant to act on the request for authority to write-off unliquidated cash advances and other receivable accounts, particularly those for deceased accountable officers, in accordance with COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.

2.19 	We recommended and Management agreed to:
Revisit the Municipality’s supply and materials management to promote proper custodianship over these inventory items, in accordance with COA Circular No. 92-386 dated October 20, 1992, Prescribing Rules and Regulations on Supply and Property Management in the Local Governments.
Create an Inspectorate Committee with adequate number of members to complete the conduct of physical inventory within the prescribed timeframe, who will coordinate with the Municipal General Services Office (MGSO) in the preparation of the Report of Physical Count of Inventories (RPCI) for every semester of a given year, in accordance with COA Circular No. 2002-003 dated June 20, 2002. Likewise, the MGSO will coordinate with the Accounting Office for the periodic reconciliation of both records, as prescribed in the said COA Circular.
Consider increasing the number of personnel in the MGSO to address the manpower constraints in the said Office and facilitate the maintenance of the required records and reports.

4.15 	We recommended and Management agreed to:
Submit the lacking documentary requirements as enumerated in Table 1 of this audit finding;
Ensure that future procurement of land/real property be duly supported with the required documents in accordance with Section 13 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012; and
Require the accountant to prepare the necessary adjusting entries to recognize the full purchase price of the purchased lots in the amounts of ₱4,111,540.00 and ₱6,500,000.00, respectively, and to submit to COA copies of the related Journal Entry Vouchers.

5.15 	We recommended and Management agreed to maximize the utilization of the current year’s 70 percent Prevention and Mitigation Fund and its continuing appropriations of the LDRRMF for the implementation of disaster management programs, projects and activities in the Municipality to ensure the attainment of the objectives of Republic Act 10121, the Philippine Disaster Risk Reduction and Management (PDRRM) Act of 2010.

6.9 	We recommended for the Management to:
Direct the Human Resource Management Officer (HRMO) to supervise the proper implementation of Municipal Ordinance No. 001-2019 dated April 10, 2019 in providing medical assistance for medical check-up of eligible officials and employees of the Municipality.
Require the Municipal Accountant to ensure the completeness of documents to support the claims for financial assistance for medical check-up/examination in compliance with the subject Municipal Ordinance.
Require the concerned persons liable to refund the said grant of financial assistance for medical check-up/examination which are not compliant with the said Municipal Ordinance.

7.11 	We recommended and Management agreed to:
Instruct the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to withhold the 10 percent retention money amounting to ₱89,915.86 relating to the Contract on the Civil Works Project for Road Gravelling - Phase I at Barangay Caloc-an, Magallanes, Agusan del Norte as part of the settlement of the pertinent notice of disallowance.
Require the Contractor and those persons involved in the preparation and approval of the Program of Work (POW) to refund the amount of ₱147,053.24 equivalent to the overstated quantity particularly Item 200 – Aggregate Subbase Course resulting in excessive contract cost as compared to the COA Cost Estimate.
Require the Contractor to rectify, without using government funds, the aggregates applied which was beyond the allowable maximum size of two (2) inches or 50 millimeter in diameter for materials passing Item 200 as specified in the subject Program of Works in compliance with DPWH Standard Specifications for Highways, Bridges and Airport, otherwise, refund the deficiencies noted during the physical inspection with an equivalent amount of ₱273,436.84 less the withheld retention money of ₱89,915.86.

8.7 	We recommended and Management agreed to require the Bids and Awards Committee and its secretariat as well as other personnel concerned to submit to COA all perfected/issued contracts and Purchase Orders within five (5) working days from its perfection/issuance, consistent with COA Circular 2009-001 dated February 12, 2009.

9.11 	We recommended and Management agreed to create a committee to formulate a policy/guideline on the grant of financial assistance clearly stating therein the eligibility criteria for the grant, the required documents to support the claim, and the amount and nature of the grant to ensure fair and equitable implementation of the program in line with the basic policy of the State of ensuring efficient, economical and effective utilization of government resources as prescribed under Section 2 of Presidential Decree 1445.

10.10 	We recommended and Management agreed to execute a Memorandum of Agreement with the concerned private financial institutions relative to the functions of the Municipality as a collection agent of the latter for the personal loan amortizations of the former’s regular employees and job order personnel to secure authority to deduct from the salaries and wages of the concerned personnel the said loan amortizations, in compliance with COA Circular                No. 86-248-A dated March 17, 1986 and to ensure appropriate provisions for the collections of administration and service fees relating thereto.

We recommended that the Municipality Mayor direct the:
a. OIC-Municipal Accountant to provide allowance for impairment on expected
losses from uncollectible or long overdue receivables pursuant to Paragraph 67
of PPSAS 29; and
b. Waterworks Supervisor to exhaust all remedies to collect the outstanding
receivables and should receivables aged at least 10 years could be proven to be
uncollectible despite efforts to collect, to request write-off of these accounts
following the guidelines provided in COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December
16, 2016.

Management’s Comment:
The OIC-Municipal Accountant and the Waterworks Supervisor commented that they will
provide allowance for impairment and will prepare the required documents for the request
for write-off of the uncollected accounts, respectively.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to
make an adjusting entry to reflect the unrecorded other bank charges amounting to
P204,685.50 in the Municipality’s books of accounts and thereafter make sure that
entries are only based on reliable and relevant source documents to facilitate complete
and fair presentation of transactions in the Financial Statements.

Management’s Comment:
The OIC-Municipal Accountant commented that she will draw an adjusting entry to correct
the affected accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
a. Waterworks Supervisor to strictly enforce the provisions of the “Kasunduan Para sa
Pagpapakabit ng Tubig,” except when contrary written policies or provisions are
issued by proper authorities or agreed upon by the parties;
b. Waterworks Supervisor to initiate the preparation and submission of the operational
plan, guidelines or manual that will govern the operations of the Magallanes Water
System for the approval of the Municipal Mayor and/or other concerned Municipal
officials;
c. OIC-Municipal Treasurer and OIC-Municipal Accountant to record separately the
collection of reconnection fees and the surcharges from the Waterworks System
Fees for a more accurate presentation of revenues in their respective records and
reports; and
d. OIC-Municipal Accountant to properly account for the operations of the Magallanes
Water System and ensure that all expenses related to its operations are charged
against its own revenues or income to facilitate effective determination by decision-
makers and stakeholders of the viability of its operations, actual financial condition
and performance.

Management’s Comment:
The OIC-Municipal Accountant commented that she will transfer the supplies inventory and
expenses from the General Fund to the special account of the Waterworks System. Also,
the Waterworks Supervisor commented that he will comply with the recommendations of the
auditor regarding the operations of the Waterworks System.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Bids and Awards Committee to:
a. submit to the Auditor written justification why the operational timeline or the
recommended earliest possible time using the minimum calendar days per
procurement activity for the 13 awarded contracts was not followed and why the
required pre-procurement and pre-bidding conferences were not conducted; and
b. henceforth adhere to the operational timeline provided in Annex “C” of the 2016
Revised IRR of Republic Act No. 9184 on the conduct of competitive biddings,
otherwise disbursements related to the procurement will be disallowed in audit.

Management’s Comment:
The BAC Chairman commented that they will adhere to the provisions of Annex “C” of the
Revised IRR of RA 9184.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Bids and Awards Committee
(BAC):
a. to submit the lacking bidding documents enumerated above to the COA Auditor for
review and evaluation and make sure that all the documentary requirements are
complied with before entering into contract with the winning bidder;
b. through the BAC Secretariat to post the Notice to Proceed and the approved
contract in the PhilGEPS to promote transparency;
c. to strictly comply with the provisions of the Revised IRR of RA 9184 in the conduct
of public bidding and awarding of contracts.

Management’s Comment:
The BAC Chairman commented that they will comply with the provisions of the Revised IRR
of RA 9184.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer, as the
Property Officer, to renew the registration with the LTO of the 13 vehicles owned by
the Municipality in compliance with Section 5 of Batas Pambansa Bilang 74 and
follow-up the release of the number plates of the seven vehicles.

Management’s Comment:
The OIC-Municipal Treasurer commented that they will take action to renew the registration
and number plates of the government vehicles to comply with the Auditor’s
recommendations.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct:
a. the OIC-Municipal Treasurer to submit written justifications on why the
unserviceable properties were disposed without first informing the COA
Auditors of the disposal;
b. the OIC-Municipal Treasurer to submit at least 20 days before the
commencement of disposal activities the program for disposal with time
schedule to the office of the COA Auditors in accordance with the provisions of
COA Circular No. 89-296; and
c. the OIC-Municipal Accountant to properly accomplish the accumulated
depreciation and carrying amount portion of the Inventory and Inspection
Report of Unserviceable Properties (IIRUP) to facilitate computation of the
gain/loss on the sale of properties.

Management’s Comment:
The OIC-Municipal Treasurer commented that they will comply with the Auditor’s
recommendations.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the BAC Secretariat to furnish the
Auditor with copies of all purchase orders and/or contracts entered into by the
Municipality, together with complete relevant supporting documents, within five
working days from execution to facilitate timely and relevant auditorial review thereof.

Management’s Comment:
The BAC Chairman commented that the Municipality will comply with the Auditor’s
recommendations on the submission of contracts and purchase orders.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Budget Officer, the
Municipal Accountant and the OIC-MDRRMO to refrain from utilizing the unexpended
balances of the LDRRMF unless the projects or programs to be financed thereby are
specified under a separate caption in the LDRRMFIP and the corresponding Annual
Investment Plan (AIP) are prepared and/or approved by the Sangguniang Bayan through
an ordinance.

Management’s Comment:
The OIC-MDRRMO commented that she will comply with the auditor’s recommendations on
the proper disbursement of unexpended LDRRMF.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. require the Head, Municipal Planning and Development Council (MPDC) to present
in the AIP the specific PPAs with complete details such as the project name,
location, and cost, among others; and
b. refrain from the practice of appropriating lump-sum amounts for various PPAs cited
in generic terms, otherwise pertinent disbursements therefor shall be suspended or
disallowed in audit unless the Municipality could submit proof of prior authority or
approval from the Sanggunian Bayan to implement the PPAs, pursuant to COA
Memorandum No. 2010-014.

Management’s Comment:
The management commented that they will review the PPAs to be charged to the 20 per
cent Development Fund to comply with the auditor’s recommendations.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Environment and
Natural Resources Officer and other concerned officials to submit a catch-up plan for
the projects covered by funding from the DILG to ensure that there will be no further
delay in their implementation.

Management’s Comment:
The management commented that they will comply with the Auditor’s recommendations.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. instruct the GAD Focal Point System to monitor the implementation of all GAD
PPAs and the budget where these PPAs will be charged, and ensure that the
implementation and charging thereof are according to the approved GAD Plan and
Budget;
b. direct the GAD Focal Person to submit to the COA Audit Team documents that will
prove that the changes made in the endorsed GAD Plan and Budget were
acknowledged, reviewed and approved by the concerned DILG Office; and
c. direct the GAD FPS to submit a training plan that will build the capacity of its
members on GAD, including their competency in the use of HGDG.

Management’s Comment:
The management commented that they will comply with the Auditor’s recommendations.

We recommended and Management agreed to require the Inventory, Appraisal and Disposal Committee to perform its duties and functions as mandated by EO No. 017-2021 to come up with accurate and reliable RPCPPE which is a very important tool in ascertaining the fairness of presentation of the PPE accounts in the FS.

We also recommended and Management agreed to avail the one-time cleansing of PPE account balances prescribed by COA Circular No. 2020-006 to establish PPE balances that are verifiable as to existence, condition and accountability.

Likewise, we recommended and the MA agreed to make the necessary adjustments in the books of accounts based on the deficiencies noted in audit and submit the journal entry vouchers (JEV) together with complete supporting documents (SD) to the AT for verification.

Further, we reiterated our previous year’s recommendation and the MA agreed to prepare and maintain complete and updated PPE records such as PPELCs and lapsing schedules to present the PPE accounts fairly in the FS.

Also, we reiterated our previous year’s recommendation and the MTO agreed to prepare and maintain complete and updated PCs for control purposes.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to:
Direct the MA to observe proper recording of ongoing projects to come up with accurate and reliable CIP account balances presented in the FS;
Require the Municipal Engineer (ME) to prepare the QRGPPA in accordance with COA Circular No. 2013-004; and
Thereafter, require the MA and the ME to reconcile their records as to the status and costs incurred related to ongoing projects of the MGM on a timely basis.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to:
Require the MT to prepare the cashbooks and RCD in accordance with Section 32 of the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs, Volume I;
Require the MTO and the OMA to perform thorough review and periodic reconciliation of the Cash – Local Treasury account balances between the GL and the cashbooks; and
Require the turnover of the cashbooks of AOs separating from the LGU prior to the issuance of clearance in order to facilitate reconciliation of records.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Maximize the utilization of the 20% DF to attain the desirable socio-economic targets and outcomes of the MGM;
Evaluate thoroughly the proposed priority development PPAs to be funded under the 20% DF as to their readiness for implementation or procurement to avoid delays or non-implementation thereof; and
Consider the appropriate timing and phasing of the implementation of development PPAs taking into account the constraints brought by the ongoing pandemic.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Enjoin the LDRRM Office to exert more effort in implementing actions and measures pertaining to all aspects of DRRM towards building a disaster-resilient LGU and communities; and
Monitor closely the implementation of all PPAs under the LDRRMF to facilitate corrective action, if any, and the timely reprogramming and reallocation of the fund for its optimum utilization.

We likewise recommended and Management agreed to prioritize the utilization of the unexpended balances of LDRRMF that were transferred to the STF to ensure that these funds are used for the intended purpose within the prescribed period.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to:
Direct the BAC, the Secretariat and the Technical Working Group to abide strictly by the standards set forth by RA No. 9184, its 2016 Revised IRR and other relevant issuances on government procurement;
Submit within five working days upon execution or issuance of all copies of perfected contracts together with the SDs as required under COA Circular 2009-001; and
Instruct the MA and the ME to observe the requirements of COA Circular No. 2012-001 and COA Memorandum No. 2005-027 on the procurement/implementation of infrastructure projects at all times to avoid suspension or disallowance in audit.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to require the Local Finance Committee to prepare and submit immediately to the NDRRMC the required Statement/s on fund utilization and Report on status of implementation of PPAs for CY 2021.  Thereafter, we recommended and Management agreed to adhere strictly to the reporting requirements of PPAs funded from disaster relief aid to achieve transparency and accountability in the use of the fund.

If these efforts prove futile, we recommend that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to facilitate the submission to the COA of the request for authority to write-off the receivables together with the necessary documents enumerated in Section 8.3 of COA Circular No. 2016-005.

Ms. Lorna Medina was discovered to be under hearing. observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to classify properly payables in accordance with the Revised Chart of Accounts for Local Government Units (RCALGU) and to refrain from accruing expenses and recognizing liabilities except when related transactions are supported by adequate and valid documentation.
observations.managementAction: Payables were classified for the year.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Budget Officer closely work together to facilitate the immediate submission of the Statement of Comparison of Budget and Actual Amounts (SCBAA) for the year ended December 31, 2020 to the Audit Team in consonance with IPSAS 24.
observations.managementAction: The SCBAA for the year was prepared and submitted together with the financial statements.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to: a. Facilitate the liquidation of all outstanding cash advances at year-end and on-time and henceforth adopt appropriate control measures to prevent accumulation of unliquidated cash advances in the succeeding years; b. Refrain from granting additional cash advances to any official or employee who still has to settle his/her prior cash advance and designate instead another employee as Special Disbursing Officer if it is necessary to make additional cash advances for special activities/projects; and c. Comply with RCALGU in recording and classifying the advances granted by the Municipality.
observations.managementAction: Advances were classified accordingly. There were no additional advances granted to AOs.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to: a. Strictly comply with Republic Acts No. 8291, 7742 and 7875 as amended by RA 9241, by ensuring that the amounts of all loan payments and/or contributions withheld are remitted in full to respective government corporations; b. Investigate the cause of the negative balances of the Due to GSIS and Pag-IBIG accounts in some months to facilitate reconciliation and adjustments in the GSIS and Pag-IBIG records of the Municipality and individual employees; and c. Establish an effective accounting control system in the recording and monitoring of the Due to GSIS, Due to Pag-IBIG and Due to PhilHealth accounts to ensure that entries made thereon are accurate, complete and reconciled with the source documents and to facilitate timely detection of errors.
observations.managementAction: Contributions to different GOCCs were remitted on time. Discrepancies were already corrected for the year. Discrepancies were properly monitored.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to record all purchases of supplies and materials as Inventory items applying the Perpetual Inventory Method, such as Welfare Goods for Distribution account for relief goods and the distributed portion to Welfare Goods Expenses or other reciprocal expenses accounts of the inventories.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal records the purchase of inventories as inventory. However, they still use the periodic inventory method in establishing the amounts of inventory at year-end.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the: a. BAC, Municipal Treasurer and other concerned personnel to stop the practice of using cash advances to directly procure and pay goods but instead ensure that all payments to suppliers are made in checks except when payment through cash advances is allowed under COA Circular No. 97-002; and b. BAC and Municipal Accountant to submit to the Audit Team the documents required under GPPB 01-2020 to establish the legal, financial and technical eligibilities of the suppliers of the 600 sacks of rice and other supplies for relief operations totaling P3,371,915.00, as well as other supporting documents to establish the validity of the transactions, otherwise the procurement will be suspended in audit.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality refrained from using advances and payments were made by checks for the year. BAC and Municipal Accountant submitted the documents required under GPPB 01-2020.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant, MSWDO and other concerned officials to submit to the Audit Team the distribution lists and acknowledgement receipts duly signed by the recipients of food packs and meal supplies distributed by the Municipality totalling P11,138,662.55, for further evaluation.
observations.managementAction: Documents submitted.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant in coordination with the Human Resource Management Officer (HRMO) to submit the duly approved Daily Time Records (DTRs) of all personnel during the months they were entitled to COVID-19 hazard pay to the Audit Team for further evaluation.
