observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
a. Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to analyze the causes of
the deficiency of P6,092,174.85 between their records and make the
necessary adjustments to establish the correct balances of the subject
receivables and deferred income accounts; and
b. Municipal Treasurer to make sure that the balances of the summary report
on CLDT are reconciled with the balances from the CLDT per barangay.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant and
other concerned officials to exert effort to locate pertinent files and gather
relevant information regarding the dormant receivables amounting to
P2,082,767.89 and send demand letters to the debtors. If these efforts prove futile,
we recommend that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to
facilitate the submission to the COA of the request for authority to write-off the
receivables together with the necessary documents enumerated in Section 8.3 of
COA Circular No. 2016-005.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to
classify payables in accordance with the Revised Chart of Accounts for Local
Government Units (RCALGU) and to refrain from accruing expenses and
recognizing liabilities except when the related transactions are supported by
adequate and valid documentation.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Budget Officer
closely work together to facilitate the immediate submission of the Statement of
Comparison of Budget and Actual Amounts (SCBAA) for the year ended
December 31, 2020 to the Audit Team in consonance with IPSAS 24.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to:
a. Facilitate the liquidation of all outstanding cash advances at year-end and
henceforth, adopt appropriate control measures to prevent accumulation of
unliquidated cash advances in the succeeding years;
b. Refrain from granting additional cash advances to any official or employee
who still has to settle his/her prior cash advance and designate instead
another employee as Special Disbursing Officer if it is necessary to make
additional cash advances for special activities/projects; and
c. Comply with RCALGU in recording and classifying the advances granted by
the Municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to:
a. Strictly comply with Republic Acts No. (RA) No. 8291, 7742 and 7875 as
amended by RA 9241, by ensuring that the amounts of all loan payments
and/or contributions withheld are remitted in full to respective government
corporations; and
b. Investigate the cause of the negative balances of the Due to GSIS and Pag-
IBIG accounts in some months to facilitate reconciliation and adjustments in
the GSIS and Pag-IBIG records of the Municipality and individual employees;
c. Establish an effective control system in the recording and monitoring of the
Due to GSIS, Due to Pag-IBIG and Due to Philhealth accounts to ensure that
entries made thereon are accurate, complete and reconciled with the source
documents and to facilitate timely detection of errors.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to
record all purchases of supplies and materials as Inventory items applying the
Perpetual Inventory Method, such as Welfare Goods for Distribution account for
relief goods; and the distributed portion to Welfare Goods Expenses or other
reciprocal expenses accounts of the recorded inventories.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:
a. BAC, Municipal Treasurer and other concerned personnel to stop the
practice of using cash advances to directly procure and pay goods but
instead ensure that all payments to suppliers are made in checks except
when payment through cash advances is allowed under COA Circular No.
97-002; and
b. BAC and Municipal Accountant to submit to the Audit Team the documents
required under GPPB 01-2020 to establish the legal, financial and technical
eligibilities of the suppliers of the 600 sacks of rice and other supplies for
relief operations totaling P3,371,915.00, as well as other supporting
documents to establish the validity of the transactions, otherwise the
procurement will be suspended in audit.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant,
MSWDO and other concerned officials to submit to the Audit Team the distribution
lists and acknowledgement receipts duly signed by the recipients of food packs
and meal supplies distributed by the Municipality totalling P11,138,662.55, for
further evaluation.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant in
coordination with the Human Resource Management Officer (HRMO) to submit the
duly approved Daily Time Records (DTRs) of all personnel during the months they
were entitled to COVID-19 hazard pay to the Audit Team for further evaluation.

We reiterated the prior year’s audit recommendation that the Municipal Mayor
direct the LDRRMO to refrain from charging against the LDRRMF programs,
projects and activities that are not related to the DRRM activities as provided
under NDRRMC-DBM-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1 dated March
25, 2013.
We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor cause the transfer from the
General Fund to the Trust Fund – Trust Liability - DRRM the amount of
P143,129.49 representing improperly charged expenditures to the LDRRMF.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Require the Municipal Accountant and the Inventory Committee to reconcile their records and reports in accordance with COA Circular No. 2020-006 in order to come up with accurate and fairly presented PPE accounts in the FS;
Instruct the MEO and the Municipal Planning and Development Office to conduct periodic reconciliation of their records on all LGU-funded projects;
Direct the Inventory Committee to exercise due diligence in the conduct of the physical count of PPE and in the preparation of RPCPPE in order to come up with accurate and reliable reports; and
Consider the creation of a GSO or hiring of additional personnel in the Office of the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer’s Office (MTO) in order to facilitate the reconciliation of PPE accounts.
We also recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to:
Prepare journal entry vouchers (JEVs) to provide depreciation for all PPE items in the books of accounts; and
Prepare and maintain complete and updated records on PPE such as PPELCs and lapsing schedules in order to present them fairly in the FS.
Further, we recommended that the MTO prepare and maintain complete and updated PCs for control purposes.

We reiterated our previous year’s recommendations and Management agreed to:
Require the Municipal Engineer to provide the Inventory Committee with complete description and cost segregation of road components for road projects to facilitate the preparation of RPCLRN;
Direct the Inventory Committee and MTO to prepare and submit the RPCLRN and RLRN, respectively, in accordance with the prescribed formats as provided under COA Circular No 2015-008; and
Require the Municipal Accountant to disclose the total road network in the Notes to the FS in accordance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2015-008.
Likewise, we reiterated our previous year’s recommendation and both the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer agreed to prepare and maintain separate subsidiary records/LRNLCs and LRNPCs, respectively, for local road projects to ensure proper recording and control of the assets.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Exert utmost effort in retrieving prior years’ records and documents to gather complete or sufficient information to support the accuracy and validity of the accounts balances presented in the FS and facilitate the collection, payment or other appropriate action; and
Request for the write-off of dormant accounts in accordance with COA Circular No. 2016-005, if applicable.
Likewise, we recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to prepare and maintain complete and updated SLs and other subsidiary records of the accounts presented in the FS in accordance with Section 10 of the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs, Volume II.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Exert utmost effort in retrieving prior years’ records related to the Due from Other Funds and Due to Other Funds accounts to support the balances presented in the FS;
Instruct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to prioritize the reconciliation of the reciprocal accounts in order to come up with accurate balances presented in the FS; and
Thereafter, request for the write-off of dormant accounts balances in accordance with COA Circular No. 2016-005.
Likewise, we recommended and Management agreed to require the Municipal Accountant to:
Review and analyze transactions thoroughly before taking up the transactions in the books and completely account for the same to prevent variances between the reciprocal accounts; and
Prepare and maintain complete and updated records of the Due from Other Funds and Due to Other Funds accounts in order to support the accuracy and reliability of the amounts presented in the FS and facilitate audit of the same.

We recommended and the MTO agreed to:
Prepare and use the RIS and SSMI in accordance with Section 121 of the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs, Volume I, in order to facilitate the timely recording of transactions related to the issuance of inventories in the books of accounts; and
Come up with internal control procedures that will ensure proper handling of inventories to prevent or minimize the risk of loss and misuse such as maintenance of logbook for expected and actual deliveries based on approved POs and delivery receipts and use of ICS for issuance of small tangible items with serviceable life of more than one year but small enough to be considered as PPE.
We also recommended and Management agreed to:
Require the Inventory Committee to exercise diligence in performing its functions and responsibilities as required in the pertinent provisions of the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs, Volume I;
Require the Inventory Committee and the Office of the Municipal Accountant to prioritize the reconciliation between the Inventories accounts balances per books and per RPCI; and
Require the MTO and the Office of the Municipal Accountant to properly prepare and maintain complete and updated SCs and SLCs, respectively, and reconcile their records regularly.
Further, we recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to prepare JEVs based on complete transaction documents presented in accordance with Section 31 of the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs, Volume II, and IPSAS 12, Inventories, in order to properly account for the acquisition and issuance of the supplies and materials inventory.
Furthermore, we recommended and the BAC Secretariat agreed to prepare the indicative and supplemental APP based on PPMPs and supplemental PPMPs, respectively, and submit the same to the GPPB and to COA in accordance with Section 7.4 of the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184.

We recommended and the Municipal Treasurer agreed to:
Prepare the Certified List of Taxpayers in order to provide the Office of the Municipal Accountant an accurate basis for the set-up of RPT and SET Receivables at the beginning of the year; and
Prepare and submit annually the Certified List of Delinquent Taxpayers to the SB to request for assistance in the enforcement of the remedies for collection of RPT and SET.
We also recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to:
Prepare and maintain the Abstract of RPT Collections in accordance with Section 20 of the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs, Volume I; and
Strictly observe due diligence and conservatism in recording transactions related to the levy of RPT and SET by using the proper accounts as provided under Annex B of COA Circular No. 2015-009 in order to come up with accurate and faithfully represented FS.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Encourage the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Office to exert more effort in implementing actions and measures pertaining to all aspects of disaster risk reduction and management towards building a disaster-resilient LGU and communities; and
Closely monitor the implementation of all PPAs under the LDRRMF to facilitate corrective action, if any, and the timely reprogramming and reallocation of the fund for its optimum utilization.
We also reiterated our previous year’s recommendation and Management agreed to direct the MSWDO to be in the frontline of service delivery during and in the aftermath of man-made and natural disasters and calamities, particularly in the disposition and utilization of relief goods.
Likewise, we recommended and Management agreed to direct the concerned officials to be meticulous in the preparation of LGU reports to ensure that accurate information are provided for decision making.

We recommended Management to:
Comply with the given procedures in the checking of eligibility requirements of the prospective bidders and observe adherence to the provisions of the abovementioned Circular and IRR;
Direct the BAC Secretariat to provide the GSO with information or certification on the current and updated eligibility documents of the winning bidders; and
Direct the Municipal Accountant to check the completeness of documentary requirements before the submission of DVs for approval of payment by the Municipal Mayor.

We recommended Management to:
Comply with the posting requirements provided under GPPB-TSO Adviser No. 05-2020 on the projects undertaken through Emergency Procurement under the Bayanihan Act.
Identify the principal and alternate authorized representatives who will be in charged in posting the required procurement information. The name, position and official email address of the said representatives shall be endorsed by the appropriate officer of the PE and submitted to the GPPB through GPPBonlineregistration@gppb.gov.ph.

Direct the MDRRMO to provide breakdown or specify the PPAs for COVID-19 in the LDRRMFIP to comply with the provisions on budgeting as required in Sections 321 and 336 of RA 7160 and Local Budget Circular No. 124 dated March 26, 2020; and
Comply to the provision of COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012 on Accounting and Reporting Guidelines for LDRRMF.  Submit an LDRRMFIP based on Annex A of the stated Circular.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant stop the charging  of expenses not related to the purpose of the DRRM Fund, taking into consideration the existing rules and regulations governing the utilization of the said fund.

Direct the Municipal Accountant to verify the nature of the PPAs whether those were constructions, simple or major repairs/rehabilitations   before preparing the necessary adjusting entries to reflect the corrections of the affected accounts; Likewise, ensure that all developmental projects to be funded under the 20% Development Fund of the Municipality shall partake the nature of investment or capital expenditures as prescribed in DILG and DBM Joint Memorandum Circular Nos. 2017-1 and 2020-1, respectively.
Ensure that the utilization of the 20 percent Development Fund or any other funds are authorized through approved appropriations and that PPAs in the AIP are specified or properly identified.

Hasten the conduct of complete physical count of PPE and Inventory, prepare the RPCPPE and RPCI and submit the same to the Accounting Office so that reconciliation of the GSO and accounting records can be attained and that the reliability of the balances of PPE and Inventory accounts in the Statement of Financial Position can be ascertained.
Direct the Municipal Accountant and Agriculturist to reconcile their records on the balance of the Biological Assets – Breeding Stocks account and prepare valid adjustments, if there are any.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
1. Direct the Municipal Accountant to comply with the provisions of
Sections 20 and 23, Chapter 3, Volume I of the Manual on the New
Government Accounting System (MNGAS) for Local Government Units
(LGUs) and Sections 233 and 235 of Republic Act (RA) in setting-up the
Real Property Tax (RPT) and Special Education Tax Receivables (SET)
as well as the Deferred RPT and SET Income accounts; and
2. Require the Municipal Accountant to rectify and reconstruct the
incorrect entries made in CY 2019 on the set-up of Real Property Taxes
(RPT) and Special Education Taxes (SET) applying the recommended
correcting entries provided in Appendix 1.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal
Accountant, in coordination with concerned officials, to initiate and facilitate the
drafting or preparation of a policy on the provision of ADA on AR-CWS, taking into
consideration the age of the accounts, collection experiences of the agency,
expected loss experiences and identified doubtful accounts, after which, make the
necessary adjustment to ensure that the AR-CWS account is presented in the
financial statements at its net realizable value.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal
Accountant to coordinate with the Provincial Accountant to verify the discrepancy
in the Due from LGU account of the Municipality with the corresponding
reciprocal account in the books of the Province and thereafter, make the
necessary adjustments.

Likewise, it was recommended that the Municipal Accountant request the
Provincial Accountant to furnish her with copies of the Journal Entry Vouchers
showing the amount collected by the Province in favor of the Municipality to serve
as basis in recording the Due from LGU in the books of the Municipality as
provided under Section 20 of the Manual on NGAS for LGUs, Volume I.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Budget
Officer to refrain from obligating and charging LDRRMF for expenditures which
are not directly related to the implementation of disaster risk management
activities and ensure strict compliance with the guidelines set forth under
NDRRMC, DBM and DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1. It is
recommended that administrative and operating expenses be charged instead
against the respective General Fund appropriations of the office concerned as
provided under NDRRMC-DILG-DBM-CSC Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2014-1
dated April 4, 2014.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal
Treasurer to increase the amount of fidelity bond of the subject accountable officers to
cover the large amount of their cash advances pursuant to Treasury Circular
No. 02-2009 to protect the interest of the Municipal Government, otherwise, limit the
amount of their cash advances up to the maximum cash accountability of their
fidelity bond.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer
to:
a) adopt stringent and additional measures such as deputizing barangay
treasurers, deploying collection caravans, conduct intensive/aggressive
tax campaign and information drive, particularly explaining the legal obligation
of every lot owner, including their heirs, to pay taxes on their property whatever
personal circumstances they may have, and the administrative and other legal
remedies that the Municipality may take to enforce its right under R.A. 7160 in
order to encourage or compel more taxpayers to settle their obligations;
b) send demand letters or Notice of Delinquencies to delinquent taxpayers
and apply the appropriate administrative and other legal remedies provided for
under R.A. 7160 against those taxpayers who will still refuse to pay their tax
liabilities despite valid demands from the Municipality;
c) direct the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to facilitate
reconciliation of the RPT/SET Receivables balances to arrive at the correct
adjusted amounts of the accounts; and
d) instruct the Municipal Treasurer to coordinate with the Municipal Assessor in
the computation of the correct Real Property Taxes (RPT) and Special Education
Taxes (SET) to be subjected for collections.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

a. instruct Municipal Officials and Employees to adhere strictly to DILG
Memorandum Circular No. 2011-59 by discouraging the conduct of or
attendance to trainings, seminars, workshop or similar activities
outside the geographical island where the concerned local government
unit is located and to exercise prudence in disbursing government
funds and to iterate these policies to seminars providers and other
institutions who send invitations to the Municipality to send
participants to said seminars;
b. submit justification/explanation why concerned Officials and
Employees were allowed to attend the above cited seminars despite the
directive of the DILG; and
c. require strict adherence to Section 6 of Executive Order No. 298 and
cause the refund of P15,120.00 representing the excess claims of per
diem.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

a. direct the Accountant to check whether the required documents are
attached to the DVs before processing the same for approval and
payment in compliance with Section 4 (6) of Presidential Decree No. 1445
and COA Circular 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012;
b. direct all concerned employees to submit all the lacking documents
listed in Appendix 1 as required in COA Circular No. 2012-001 for further
review and evaluation of the Audit Team, otherwise a Notice of
Suspension (NS) will be issued in accordance with the Rules and
Regulations on Settlement of Accounts (RRSA).

Require the MTO and OMA to prioritize the reconciliation process and ensure that discrepancies noted are immediately investigated and adjusted in the books accordingly; and
Require the OMA to maintain complete and updated PPELCs to support GL account balances and the MTO to keep corresponding PCs for control purposes.

Further, we recommended and the OMA agreed to continue its efforts in tracing back prior years’ acquisitions to obtain a complete details of the PPE accounts that will serve as basis in the computation of depreciation, in order to present true value of the depreciable PPE in the FS.

Require the Municipal Engineer to provide the Inventory Committee with complete description and cost segregation of road components for road projects to facilitate the preparation of RPCLRN;
Direct the Inventory Committee and MTO to prepare and submit the RPCLRN and RLRN, respectively, in accordance with the prescribed formats as provided under COA Circular No. 2015-008; and
Require the Municipal Accountant to disclose the total road network in the Notes to the FS in accordance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2015-008.

We also recommended and both the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer agreed to prepare and maintain separate subsidiary records/LRNLCs and LRNPCs, respectively, for local road projects to ensure proper recording and control of the assets.

Develop a concrete plan that will articulate the PPA, timeline, expected deliverables for each phase, key milestones, etc.; and
Adopt strategies to maximize the utilization of the 20 per cent Development Fund and implement the PPAs within the targeted timeframe to achieve the social, economic and environmental outcomes essential in the attainment of the constitutional objective of a desired quality of life for all.

We also recommended and Management agreed to ensure that the 20 per cent Development Fund is used solely for development PPAs and no amount is utilized for expenditure items not allowed to be charged thereof as provided in DILG and DBM JMC No. 2017-1.

Ensure that the basic requirements for the contract time extension and/or work suspension are complied with in accordance with Annex E of the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 and that proper documentation is observed at all times to warrant the propriety of the transactions of the Municipality; and
Observe the imposition of liquidated damages to any contractor who refuses to satisfactorily complete the work within the specified contract time, plus any extension duly granted and is considered in default under the contract.

Strictly adhere to the guidelines on the implementation of the cash-for-work program as provided in DSWD Administrative Order No. 15, Series of 2008; and
Warrant that all disbursements are supported with proper documentation prior to payment to avoid future suspension or disallowance in audit.

Require the Municipal Accountant to properly account for the receipt and issuance of relief goods based on IPSAS 12, Inventories, and make the necessary adjustments to reflect the correct balance of affected inventory and expense accounts.

We also reiterated our previous year’s recommendation and Management agreed to direct the MSWDO to be in the frontline of service delivery during and in the aftermath of man-made and natural disasters and calamities, particularly in the disposition and utilization of relief goods.

Strictly adhere to the guidelines on the acquisition of second-hand/reconditioned transport equipment as provided in NBC No. 446-A. Likewise, we recommended and Management agreed to facilitate the registration of the said vehicles under the name of the Municipality.

1.6	We recommended Management to direct the Municipal Accountant to coordinate with the depository banks for the prompt release of monthly Bank Statements in order to facilitate the timely preparation and submission of Bank Reconciliation Statements of all depository accounts maintained by the Municipality in compliance with the provisions of Section 3.2 of COA Circular No. 96-011 dated October 2, 1996.

2.9	We recommended management and agreed to:
a.	Direct the Municipal Accountant to issue Demand Letters to the concerned debtors/accountable officers for the liquidation of their accountabilities;
b.	Initiate collection measures to recover the aging accounts from persons liable who were already terminated from the government service or no longer connected with the Municipal Government; and
c.	Impose stricter control in the grant and liquidation of cash advances to ensure compliance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002.
2.10	The Management commented and we quote:
“A number of long-term, past due accounts constitute mostly of cash advances amount drawn by accountable officers/employees who are no longer connected with the Municipality, enabling us to locate and contact them with difficulty. However, we will endeavor to conform to the recommendations set by the COA by issuing demand letters to the said concerned debtors in order to collect their liabilities. Furthermore, this Office has already imposed stringent control with regard to the grant and liquidation of cash advances to officials/employees.”

3.7	We recommended Management and they agreed to:
a.	Strictly comply with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016 on the required analysis of dormant accounts by performing the conduct of regular and periodic verification, analysis, and validation of the existence of the receivables and unliquidated cash advances, and determine the concerned debtors, accountable officers for strict monitoring and disposition of accounts.
b.	File request for authority to write-off dormant receivable accounts, unliquidated cash advances, and fund transfers to the Office of the Auditor.
3.8	The Management committed to comply with the audit recommendations.

4.10	We recommended the Management to direct the Municipal Accountant exert extra effort to reconcile the reciprocal accounts in all funds maintained by the Municipality by identifying the causes of discrepancies in the corresponding subsidiary ledgers and to effect immediate, proper and verifiable adjustments in the books of accounts to reflect the correct account balances, thus, ensuring the presentation of reliable data on the financial reports of the Municipality. Likewise, periodic reconciliation of the accounts should be made to ensure the accuracy of the reciprocal accounts.
4.11 	Management commented that reconciliation of the Due from/Due to Other Funds is a complicated process since proper monitoring and recording of accounts was not practiced by the former accountant. Further, the Municipal Accountant reasoned out that the amount disclosed in the books were for reporting purposes only and should have therefore be eliminated in the financial report. Likewise, she acknowledged her failure to reconcile the balances and assured the Audit Team that they will be more cautious in the recognition of accounts from now on.

5.6	We recommended the Management that for purposes of presenting of intra-Agency transactions in the consolidated Financial Statements, eliminate reciprocal accounts.
5.7	Management commented that they will follow the elimination process of reciprocal accounts in the next financial year.

6.7	We recommended and Management committed to:
a.	Require the MGSO-Designate to submit immediately the Reports on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment to the Office of the Auditor as required in Sec. 124 of NGAS Manual, Vol. I; and
b.	Instruct the MGSO-Designate to prepare and maintain complete and updated property cards and require to reconcile with the records of the Municipal Accountant regularly pursuant to Sec. 114 of the same Manual.
6.8	Management commented that they will recreate an Inventory Committee. Also, the MGSO committed to exert extra effort to fully accomplish the physical count of PPE and to prepare and maintain complete and updated property cards so that reconciliation of records and ledger cards with the Accounting unit will be made regularly.

7.6	We recommended Management to instruct the Municipal Accountant to strictly comply with the provisions of Section 99 of Presidential Decree 1445 on the reversion of the unexpended balance to the unappropriated surplus of the general fund.
7.7	Management committed to issue a Sanggunian Resolution to revert these unexpended LGU counterpart to the unappropriated surplus of the General Fund so that funds would be made available for other essential purposes.

8.6	We recommended Management to direct the Municipal Accountant to:
Maintain subsidiary ledger for the transfers of agency’s unutilized DRRMF to the special trust fund by year of transfer;
b.	Gather documents and other data available to trace balances of LDRRMF under the STF Account which are due for reversion to the unappropriated surplus of the General Fund.
8.7	Management commented and we quote:
“The Office of the Municipal Accountant will comply with the recommendations. We will maintain subsidiary ledgers for the transfer of unutilized MDRRMF to the Special Trust Fund and collect data to trace balances of MDRRMF under STF account which are due for reversion to the unappropriated surplus of the General Fund.”

9.5	We recommended Management to Request Sanggunian Bayan to issue a resolution to open separate bank accounts for each EEs in order for the Management to improve cash management with regards to these enterprises, and to reach a sound economic decision.
9.6	This is an Interim Audit finding which was already complied by the Management.

10.5	We recommended and Management committed to:
a.	Direct the Municipal Accountant to institute a mechanism such as assigning personnel which reports directly to her so that submission of accounts can be properly monitored; and
b.	Henceforth, follow strictly the regulations in the submission of accounts.
10.6	This is an Interim Audit finding and was fully implemented by Management.

11.6	We recommended Management to:
a.	Direct the Municipal Accountant to cause the immediate submission of all paid DVs with original supporting documents to the Auditor’s Office;
b.	Henceforth, follow strictly the regulations of the submission of accounts.
11.7	This is an Interim Audit finding and was fully implemented by Management.

12.5	We recommended and Management committed to:
a.	Strictly enforce complete and proper accomplishment of Driver’s Trip Ticket to support payments of gasoline, oil and lubricants to minimize wasteful, excessive and unnecessary expenditures; and
b.	Comply with the preparation and submission of the Monthly Consumption Report and Monthly Report of Official Travel, as prescribed under COA Circular No. 77-61.
12.6	Management commented that they have already started generating the required monthly reports and assured the Audit Team that complete and proper accomplishment of the DTTs will now be strictly observed.

13.8	We recommended Management and they committed to:
a.	Submit justification/explanation why the reprogramming of appropriations was not covered by an approved ordinance covering a supplemental budget; and
b.	Ensure that reprogramming of PPAs are done by way of appropriation ordinance covering a supplemental budget and duly submitted to the Sangguniang Panlalawigan for review.
13.9	Management commented that the LGU assumed that Resolutions would suffice the requirement of the law, however, they assured the Audit Team that they will issue an Appropriation Ordinance the next time they will reprogram one PPA to another in accord with the audit recommendation.

14.8	We recommended to management and they committed to refrain from charging non-related expenses to the MDRRM Fund and comply with the above-stated regulation in prioritizing expenses.
14.9	The Management commented that the disbursements totaling ₱218,746.25 that were charged to MDRRM Fund were not contrary to Sec (3)a Rule 1 of the IRR of RA 10121 since these were utilized for the operation of the MDRRM Office. Also, these travelling expenses were for the attendance to National and Local Trainings related to Disaster Preparedness. Thus, disbursements made were to support the MDRRM programs and activities.
Further, the insufficiency of the General Fund to cover the expenses to meet the criteria to qualify in the Gawad Kalasag Program caused MDRRMO to charge these expenses to the 5% MDRRMF.
In addition, the SB of Magallanes has already crafted the ordinance for the creation of the LDRRM Office to clear things out and to abide by the institutionalization of the MDRRMO.

15.7	We recommended Management to direct the Municipal Budget Officer to establish separate RAAO for QRF, MOOE and CO of the LDRRMF as provided in Section 5.1.4 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012. In addition, require the Municipal Accountant to account for the CO transferred to the special trust fund account and revert the same to the General Fund as continuing.
15.8	The Municipal Budget Officer commented that they have already established separate RAAO for QRF, MOOE and CO at present in compliance with the audit recommendation.

16.8	We recommended Management and they committed to:
a.	Continue with the Information Education Campaign (IEC) and cooperate with the barangays and other sectors of the society for the proper implementation of waste segregation according to waste classification; and
b.	Provide measure to prevent disposal of unsegregated wastes in the TRCA, particularly during evening, and strictly impose fines and penalties to those who violates.
16.9	The Management commented that at present, the MENRO has already deployed personnel during nigh time to overlook and prevent disposal of unsegregated wastes in the TRCA. Likewise, the Office has conducted a house-to-house demonstration to eight (8) barangays and three (3) private companies regarding proper waste segregation and disposal thereof. Further, at present, the issuance of citation tickets has been strictly enforced by the Municipality in cases wherein RA 9003 is violated.

17.8	We recommended and Management committed to ensure that the SEF shall be utilized solely for its purpose; i.e., programs, projects and activities enumerated in Section 272 of RA 7160, DECS, DBM and DILG Joint Circular No. 01-B dated June 25, 2001 and DepEd, DBM and DILG Joint Circular No. 1, s. 2017.
17.9	Management admitted that the Appointments of their two (2) job order employees were corrected to “Utility/Janitorial Staff” only last August 2019. They reasoned out that the employees concerned were performing utility functions such as menial segregation of garbage, facilitate its disposal and maintaining the cleanliness of comfort rooms; and that their involvement in clerical activities were done only upon necessity i.e. maternity leave of the Administrative Assistant. Nonetheless, the Management ensured to strictly observed the guidelines on SEF utilization.

18.6	We recommended to management and they committed to:
a.	Conduct training/workshop on how to administer the HGDG tool or hire an expert that could perform gender analysis using the said tool; and
b.	Require the GAD Focal Point System to administer the HGDG in the GAD Plan and Budget, and GAD Accomplishment Report.
18.7	The MSWD Officer commented that the inability of the Municipality to administer HGDG tool was due to the time constraint since he assumed position only on December 2019, however, the MSWDO assured the Audit Team that they will prioritize the conduct of HGDG-related trainings and that they are hopeful to fully administer the tool this year in their GAD Plan and Budget and GAD Accomplishment Report.

19.6	We recommended the Management to:
a.	Limit the payroll deductions to the order of preference provided in the GAA and ensure that an employee’s net take home pay shall not be less than the minimum allowed take home pay of ₱5,000.00 as provided under Section 52 of RA 11260; and
b.	Cease from being a withholding agent on loan obligations of employees in lending institutions that are not among those specified in the provisions of the applicable GAA.
19.7	Management commented and we quote:
“Magallanes Municipal Employees Multi-Purpose Cooperative (MMEMPC) is in fact an association that is identified under the General Appropriations ACT (GAA) FY 2019 as provided in Section 52 re “b. Non-stock savings and loan associations and mutual benefit associations duly operating under existing laws and cooperatives which are managed by and/or for the benefit of government employees”. Therefore, this Municipality may act as a withholding agent on loan obligations of employees at MMEMPC. On the other hand, we will not allow the said 22 employees to have a net take home pay below ₱5,000.00 in the ensuing payroll months despite being MMEMPC an authorized lending institution.”

20.6	We recommended Management to request the concerned Local Officials to review the Revenue Code of the Municipality of Magallanes and provide amendments, if necessary, in accordance with the pertinent provisions of the Section 191 of RA 7160.
20.7	Management committed to comply with the audit recommendation.

21.6	We recommended Management to request from local sanggunian an appropriate ordinance authorizing the operations of its revenue generating activities.
21.7	Management committed to comply with the audit recommendation.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: a) Instruct the concern officials to exert extra effort in securing all pertinent documents to facilitate the registration and transfer of the title properties from the previous owners to the Municipality of Magallanes to ensure legality of ownership and security from third party claims; b) Require the Municipal Accountant to facilitate the recording in the LGU’s books and make the necessary disclosure in the financial statements; c) Secure tax assessment for the remaining lots from the BIR to know the unpaid transfer taxes due and for the Municipality to allot budget to facilitate payment of the same; and d) Make disclosures in the Notes to Financial Statements as regards the status of the titling of the acquired/donated parcels of land.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management:

Exercise prudence on the use of scarce financial resources and comply strictly with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2012-003 on the prevention of the incurrence of irregular, unnecessary, excessive, extravagant and unconscionable expenditures or uses of government funds and property;

Encourage the concerned LGU officials entitled to representation allowance that expenses incidental to and in connection with the actual performance of their functions such as hosting of official meetings, conferences and entertainments are properly charged against the said allowance; and

Adhere to the provisions of the 2016 Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations (IRR) of Republic Act (RA) No. 9184 particularly on the modes of procurement to be adopted by the Municipality on all its procurement activities to avoid future suspension or disallowance in audit.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management require the:

AOs to immediately remit/settle their overdue unremitted collections.




Municipal Treasurer to subsequently deposit the same to the agency’s depository account and strictly comply with Sections 69 and 111 of PD No. 1445.

Furthermore, we recommended that Management install necessary measures to strengthen the internal control in the collecting system of the Municipality as follows:

The Municipal Treasurer should require the collectors to maintain cashbook to record collections and remittances to the Liquidating Officer for easy monitoring and supervision.

Require the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to reconcile their records and make adjustments, if any, every end of the month.

observations.reason: Unremitted collections were not fully remitted/settled. Also, one of the AOs has retired and now deceased, thus, probability of collection is nil.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management require the Municipal Treasurer to regularly update the cashbooks of the Cash in Bank accounts for proper and timely monitoring of the balances of each bank accounts of the Municipality, in order to avoid the incurrence of bank service charges. The concerned AOs shall refund the amount of ₱17,317.00 debited by the government depository bank from the LGU’s bank accounts for bank service charges due to their negligence.

We likewise recommended that the Municipal Treasurer return and deposit the said amount to the National Treasury or ask an authority from the funder to use the trust fund.  For the meantime, the dormant accounts could be closed, transfer to an active account and maintain appropriate SLs to identify the unutilized funds for BEMOC and KR-2 projects to avoid incurrence of bank service charges.
observations.reason: The updating is not regularly observed due to voluminous workload.

We likewise recommended that the concerned Municipal officials stop approving the loan applications of employees with net take home pay below the minimum balance required by the GAA during the year.
observations.reason: Some regular employees still receive a net take home pay lower than the required net take home pay.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a) Instruct the concern officials to exert extra effort in securing all pertinent
documents to facilitate the registration and transfer of the title of the land
properties from the previous owners to the Municipality of Magallanes to ensure
legality of the ownership and security from third party claims;
b) Require the Municipal Accountant to facilitate the recording in the books of the
five unrecorded lots as part of the PPE – Land to present the reliable and correct
balance of the account to be presented in the financial statements;
c) Secure tax assessment for the remaining lots from the BIR to know the unpaid
transfer taxes due and for the Municipality to allot budget to facilitate payment of
the same; and
d) Make disclosures in the Notes to Financial Statements as regards the status of the
titling of the acquired/donated parcels of land.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to
reconstruct the unrecognized depreciation expenses of the PPE – Local Road
Networks and PPE – Other Public Infrastructures starting from the year that the
assets were recognized and make the necessary adjusting entries to Government
Equity and Accumulated Depreciation accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. Instruct the Municipal Accountant and the CWS Officer-In-Charge to determine
the correct and accurate beginning balance of Outstanding Accounts
Receivables (water sales) and adjust the books accordingly, if needed;
b. Require the Municipal Accountant to apply the Accrual Basis Accounting in the
recognition of the Municipality’s monthly water sales income from the CWS
Operation to recognize revenues to the year/period they relate;
c. Require the CWS Officer-In-Charge to furnish the Municipal Accountant the
monthly water sales (billing) to facilitate the recording and recognition of the
monthly accounts receivables and water sales income; and
d. Advise the CWS Officer-In-Charge to request and seek updates of the CWS
computerized system so it could generate the balance of Accounts Receivable at
any given period.

We recommended that the Municipality fully utilize the balance of the 20%
Development Fund and implement the remaining projects or reprogram those that are
not anymore considered as priority so that the intended beneficiaries could derive the
optimum benefits from the Fund.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to ensure
that payments for supplies, fuels, oils and lubricants, repair and maintenance and
other operating expenses, be made directly to the suppliers/service providers by
check in accordance with Section 93 of PD No. 1445 and instruct concern Officers
and Employees to adopt the alternative modes of procurement, except for emergency
cases, pursuant to the provisions of Section 48 of RA No. 9184 to minimize
reimbursement of procurement transactions.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a) Require the Municipal Accountant and General Services Officer to maintain SLC
and SC for CWS’s supplies and materials account in compliance with Section 114
of Chapter 7 of the MNGAS and to facilitate periodic reconciliation and monitoring
of the movement of supplies and materials balances and availability thereof; and
b) Instruct the General Services Officer to prescribe the use of RIS by any
requisitioning office as basis for issuance of the requested supplies and materials
and prepare the weekly consolidated on issued/used supplies and materials as
provided by Sections 116 and 121 of Chapter 7 of MNGAS.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to pass
the necessary Adjusting Journal Entry (AJE) to present the correct balances of the Other
Accounts Payable and CIP Accounts and to strictly adhere to the provisions of
Paragraph 7 of Philippine Public Sector Accounting Standards (PPSAS) No. 1
specifically in the proper accrual/ recognition of assets and liabilities to fairly present the
balances of the accounts in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require:
a. The Municipal Treasurer to send written notices to the payees of the existence of
their checks subject for replacement and do the same one (1) month prior to the
date when the check issued will become stale as provided by Section 59 of the
MNGAS, Volume I; and
b. The Municipal Accountant to pass the necessary adjusting journal entries for the
cancellation of the stale checks and restoration of the accounts payable as
shown in the above pro-forma entries prescribed under Section 61 (6) of the
MNGAS, Volume I.

We recommended that Management:
Allocate adequate budget for the titling of the subject land properties in the name of the Municipality to establish absolute ownership and reduce the risk of third party claims; and
Conduct proper evaluation of the 72 RPUs to facilitate the immediate recognition as assets of the Municipality considering the criteria provided under PPSAS 17.

We recommended that Management:
Seek authority from the Sangguniang Bayan (SB) for the Local Chief Executive to transfer the various PPE being used by other government agencies and facilitate the subsequent transfer to them; and
Thereafter, submit the relevant documents to the OMA for the dropping of the subject properties in accordance with COA Circular No. 92-386.

We recommended and Management agreed to require the:
Municipal Treasurer to furnish the Municipal Accountant a certified list of taxes due and collectible based on RPTAR/Taxpayer’s index card at the beginning of each year as basis in establishing the correct RPT and SET receivables;
Municipal Accountant to maintain and update SLs of RPT and SET Receivables and reconcile the same with the records of the Municipal Treasurer to come up with accurate and reliable RPT and SET Receivable account balances; and
Municipal Accountant to properly account the discounts based on the sharing prescribed under the Local Government Code and record fines and penalties charged on delinquent RPT and SET using the Tax Revenue - Fines and Penalties - Property  Taxes account (4-01-05-020).
We likewise recommended that Management direct the Municipal Assessor to conduct a general revision of assessments and property classification in order to update the value of all real properties situated in the locality.

We recommended and Management agreed to exhaust all possible means to require the liquidation or settlement of cash advances including, but not limited to, the following:
Demand from the concerned AOs the immediate submission of liquidation documents and/or refund of the cash advance if not yet utilized; and
Institute appropriate criminal or administrative sanctions for the continued non-compliance with the existing laws, rules and regulations on the granting, utilization and liquidation of cash advances, if warranted.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Exercise prudence on the use of scarce financial resources and comply strictly with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2012-003 on the prevention of the incurrence of irregular, unnecessary, excessive, extravagant and unconscionable expenditures or uses of government funds and property;
Encourage the concerned LGU officials entitled to representation allowance that expenses incidental to and in connection with the actual performance of their functions such as hosting of official meetings, conferences and entertainments are properly charged against the said allowance; and
Adhere to the provisions of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 particularly on the modes of procurement to be adopted by the Municipality on all its procurement activities to avoid future suspension or disallowance in audit.

We recommended and Management agreed to require the Municipal Accountant to exert effort in confirming the loan balance with the DOF so that proper disposition of the said balance may be effected in the books.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to present the current portion of the Loans Payable - Domestic amounting to ₱1.40 million in the Current Liabilities section of the SFPo as of December 31, 2018 and consistently apply the guidelines prescribed by PPSAS on the Presentation of Financial Statements.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Require the Municipal Budget Officer and the Municipal Accountant to review their records and effect immediately the necessary adjustments in their respective reports; and
Instruct the Municipal Budget Officer and the Municipal Accountant to conduct a periodic reconciliation of their records to come up with a more accurate and reliable reporting on the utilization of the 20 per cent Development Fund.

We recommended and Management agreed to strictly comply with DBM and DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No.  2017-1, by taking into consideration the following:
Develop a concrete plan that will articulate the PPA, timeline, expected deliverables for each phase, key milestones, etc.; and
Adopt strategies to maximize the utilization of the 20 per cent Development Fund and implement the PPAs within the targeted timeframe to achieve the social, economic and environmental outcomes essential in the attainment of the constitutional objective of a desired quality of life for all.

We recommended and Management agreed to require the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant to exert extra effort in preparing and submitting on time the subject accounts and financial reports including the supporting documents. We also recommend that such activities be properly monitored so that reports are submitted promptly in accordance with the salient provisions of COA Circular Nos. 2009-006 and 2002-003.

observations.recommendation:
We recommend that Management require the HRMO to:
a) formulate plan and issue policy guidelines on the hiring of casuals/contractual/job order personnel in accordance with Section 2, Rule XI of CSC Memorandum Circular No. 40 series of 1998, COA rules and regulations and existing labor and tax laws to strengthen internal controls pertaining thereto;
b) prepare  individual job  order contracts stating the job descriptions which are specific in nature as to the duties and responsibilities, targeted quantity of work to be done during the contact period and particular office to be assigned.
c) determine and plan further on how to achieve an ideal and realistic personnel complement of the Municipality;
We also recommend that Management require the Municipal Accountant, HRMO and the department heads concerned to see to it that all payrolls for the wages of job order personnel be adequately supported by:
a. for those assigned in the field, office orders and certificate of appearances must be attached, in accordance with Section 4(6) of P.D. 1445 stating that all claims against government funds shall be supported with complete documentation.
b. a certification by the Local Chief Executive/HRMO that the activities/services cannot be provided by regular or permanent personnel of the Municipality.
observations.managementAction: Followed-up status with the concerned offices.
observations.reason:
The Municipality did not formulate policy guidelines on the hiring of casual/contractual/job order personnel.
The Municipality did not prepare individual job order contracts.
Lack of personnel to perform the task.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal
Accountant to reconcile the records of the Accounting with that of the Treasury and
make the necessary adjustment to reflect the correct balance of Real Property Tax
(RPT) and Special Education Tax (SET) Receivables and Deferred RPT and SET
income based on the certified list of taxpayers submitted by the Municipal
Treasurer.

We recommended that the Municipality fully utilize the balance of the 20%
Development Fund and implement the remaining projects or reprogram those that
are not anymore considered as priority so that the intended beneficiaries could
derive the optimum benefits from the Fund.

Officer and GAD Focal Person to ensure that only PPAs that address the identified
GAD issues and concerns be included and charged to the GAD Plan and Budget,
respectively.

Engineering Office and the BAC Secretariat to furnish the Audit Team with copies
of approved contracts and all the documents forming part thereof by reference or
incorporation within five days from their execution as prescribed under COA
Circular No. 2009-001 dated February 12, 2009, to facilitate the prompt review and
evaluation of the contracts and the early communication to management on the
deficiencies noted, if any.

ensure that the required PPMP and APP for Infrastructure Projects are prepared
and submitted before any procurement of goods, services and civil works is
undertaken to properly plan and monitor the priority projects of the Municipality
for a particular budget year.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant properly prepare the Cash
Flow Statement to meet the objective as provided in the stated standard.

We recommended that Management stop the practice of paying CNA incentives without proper and complete supporting documents and strictly adhere to the rules and regulations stated in DBM Budget Circular No. 2016-7.We likewise recommended that the concerned Municipal employees and Job Order personnel refund the CNA incentives totaling P2,924,481.90, net of tax.

We recommended that Management require the: a. Accountable Officers (AOs) to immediately remit/settle their overdue unremitted collections.
b. Municipal Treasurer to subsequently deposit the same to the agency’s depository account and strictly comply with Section 69 and 111 of PD 1445.
c. Municipal Accountant to record the unrecorded collections in the books of accounts.
Furthermore, we recommended that Management install necessary measures to strengthen the internal control in the collecting system of the Municipality as follows:
a. All Revenue Collectors be required to remit their collection in full at the close of each business day as required in the above-cited provision.
b. The Liquidating Officer/Municipal Treasurer should strictly verify the individval RCD of Revenue Collectors, check the Statement of Accountable Forms and affix her signature on the back of the triplicate copy of the last Official Receipt issued to ensure that collections are remitted in full.
c. The Municipal Treasurer should require the personnel preparing the Monthly Report-for Accountable-Forms-(MRAAF)to review the report for its accuracy and completeness so that it would reflect the true accountability of each AO.
d. The Municipal Treasurer should require the collectors to maintain cashbook to record collections and remittances to the Liquidating Officer for easy monitoring and supervision.
e. Require the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to reconcile their records and make adjustments, if any, every end of the month.
f. Job Order (JO) personnel should not be designated as Accountable Officer since JO is not considered as employee of the LGU and not qualify for securing fidelity bond.

the Municipal Accountant to draw the necessary adjusting entry

We also recommended that accrual basis of accounting be applied for the preparation of Statement of Financial Performance of the CWS.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and the Municipal Engineer to submit justification/basis on the computation of ABC at 12% for evaluation of the Audit Team.  We also recommended to adopt Section 22 of Revenue Regulation No. 4-2007 issued by the Bureau of Internal Revenue in the preparation of Approved Budget for the Contract (ABC) to the advantage of the Municipal Government.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor advise the Municipal Health Officer to conduct physical inventory of drugs and medicines kept/stored in the Rural Health Unit and submit the inventory report thereon to the Municipal Treasurer/General Service Officer and Municipal Accountant for complete and accurate balance of inventory at year-end.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor advise the Municipal Planning and Development Officer and GAD Focal Person to establish and maintain GAD database as required under JMC No. 2013-01. Then, analyze and assesses the database/ sex disaggregated data and identify the priority gender issues that needs to be addressed.
We also recommended Municipal Mayor advise the Municipal Budget Officer and GAD Focal Person to ensure that only PPAs that address the identified GAD issues and concerns be included and charged to the GAD Plan and Budget.

We recommended that the Chairman of the Bids and Awards Committee ensure that the Performance Security Bond posted by the Winning Bidder is in accordance with the forms and amount prescribed under the Procurement Manual for LGUs.  If the cash bond is not yet release to the winning bidder, we also recommended for the additional performance security bond be posted to meet the minimum amount of performance security bond.

We recommended that additional warranty be posted by the contractor for the above-cited infrastructure projects to meet the minimum amount of warranty.  Henceforth, the contractor should post the necessary warranty security as prescribed under Section VI.XVII.B, Chapter V of the Procurement Manual for LGUs.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor advise the Municipal Engineer/Inspectorate Committee to issue the Certificate of Final Acceptance/Turnover after the one (1) year defect liability period so that the contractors are still accountable for any defects during the said period.
We also recommended that the performance security be released after the lapse of the one-year defect liability period, provided further that a warranty bond be issued/renewed every year until the end of the warranty period up to 15 years from the Final Acceptance depending on the category of the project.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor advise the Municipal Social Welfare and Development Officer to reassess whether or not the family of the student beneficiaries belongs to the low-income bracket of the Municipality so that only qualified and deserving students are included in the programs and abide by the guidelines on the grant of scholarship and educational assistance prescribed under the Municipal Ordinance No. 003, series of 2013 and guidelines on the grant of educational assistance.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and the Municipal Treasurer coordinate with the management of LBP-Trece Martires City on how to avail the automated payroll system so that advantages/benefits may be enjoyed by the Municipality.

We recommended that Management direct the Revenue Collectors to immediately record all daily transactions and have them totaled, balanced, closed, footed and certified at the end of each month in accordance with the abovementioned regulation. c. exert extra effort to locate the whereabouts of accountable officers who
are no longer in the service and issue final demand letters; and

d. comply strictly with the provision of COA Circular No. 97-002 dated
February 10, 1997 on the granting, utilization and liquidation of cash
advances.

We likewise recommended that Management require the Municipal
Accountant to trace the entries made that resulted in the negative balances of the
cash advance balances and effect the necessary adjusting entries in the agency books
in order to present the correct amount of the Cash Advances accounts in the
financial statements; and make appropriate action to those who did not liquidate
their cash advances within the prescribed period or render account of their
accountabilities after demand pursuant to CSC Memorandum Circular No. 12,s.
2012.

We reiterated our prior year’s audit recommendation that Management stop
the practice of charging to LDRRMF expenses which are not related to and do not
support disaster risk management activities. We likewise recommended that
Management require the Budget Officer to appropriate fund in the General Fund to
restore the amount of LDRRMF utilized for the items procured not related to
disaster risk management pursuant to Section 21 of RA 10121 and COA Circular
No. 2012-02, otherwise the payment shall be disallowed in audit. Further, we
recommended that Management requirethe Municipal Officials to adhere strictly to
the guidelines set under Section 21 of RA 10121 and COA Circular No. 2012-02 in
the appropriation and utilization of LDRRMF.

We reiterated our prior year’s audit recommendation that Management
require the Municipal Accountant to:

a) transfer the unexpended/unobligated balance of the QRF and the
DRRMF MOOE to the Special account in the Trust Fund books and
maintain subsidiary ledgers to account for transfers by year; and

b) disclose in the Notes to the Financial Statements the amount and details
of the unutilized LDRRMF in accordance with the provisions of COA
Circular No. 2012-002.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Treasurer to
regularly update the cashbooks of the Cash in Bank accounts for proper and timely
monitoring of the balances of each bank accounts of the Municipality, in order to
avoid the incurrence of bank service charges. The concerned accountable officers
shall refund the amount of $17,317.00 debited by the government depository bank
from the LGU’s bank accounts for bank service charges due to their negligence.

We likewise recommended that the Municipal Treasurer return and deposit
the said amount to the National Treasury or ask an authority from the funder to use
the trust fund. For the meantime, the dormant accounts could be closed, transfer to
an active account and maintain appropriate subsidiary ledgers to identify the
unutilized funds for BEMOC and KR-2 projects to avoid incurrence of bank service
charges.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Treasurer, the
Human Resource Management Officer (HRMO) and the Municipal Accountant to
strictly comply with the General Provisions of the General Appropriations Act and
adopt control measures to ensure that the Municipal employees’ net salary pay will
not be lower than the required maintaining balance after deducting the mandatory
contributions and other obligations. We likewise recommended that the concerned
Municipal officials stop approving the loan applications of employees with net take
home pay below the minimum balance required by the GAA during the year.

We recommended that Management require the concerned official/s to
monitor the implementation of the projects and programs under the 20 per cent
development fund and ensure its optimal utilization. We likewise recommended that
the Municipal Planning and Development Office incorporate in the AIP the detailed
or complete description of the projects to be undertaken under the said fund. Also,
proposals for development projects to the National Government Agencies should be
properly planned and submitted completely so that funds shall be downloaded
within the time frame as targeted during the year.

We reiterated our prior year’s audit recommendation that Management
adhere to existing rules and regulations on the utilization of the 20 per cent
Development Fund to ensure sufficient funding for all the programs and to achieve
the purpose for which the fund was created. We likewise recommended that
Management require the Budget Officer to appropriate fund in the General Fund to
restore the amount of 20 per cent Development Fund utilized for the items procured
which is not related to and/or not connected with the implementation of
development programs, projects and activities under the 20 per cent Development
Fund, otherwise the payment shall be disallowed in audit.
